Page ii
How to Bid
Internet Absentee or Proxy Bidding
Place absentee bids now at www.StacksBowers.com or use our iOS or Android apps on your mobile device or 
tablet. Proxy bidding is enabled at midnight the day before the auction session starts live and is located on the 
lot description page. Visit our Frequently Asked Questions page at www.StacksBowers.com/FAQ/OnlineBid-
ding.aspx for more information. 
Stack’s Bowers Galleries iBid Live
Bid live anywhere in the world, on your computer at www.StacksBowers.com or use our iOS or Android apps 
on your mobile device or tablet.
Live Telephone Bidding
Phone Bidding must be arranged at least 24 hours before your session begins. Stack’s Bowers Galleries will ask 
for the lot numbers of interest and will call you during the auction so you can place bids with our representa-
tive in real time. Phone bidding is only offered for lots estimated at €2,500 or more. If you wish to arrange live 
bidding by phone, contact our Client Services Department at +1.800.458.4646 or +1.949.253.0916 or email 
info@StacksBowers.com. 
Email and Mail Bidding
Use the enclosed bid sheet and email or mail it to us. Email bids to info@StacksBowers.com . If sending by 
mail, please allow sufficient time for the postal service. Please call to confirm that your bids have been re-
ceived by Stack’s Bowers Galleries.
Mail: A ttn. Client Services Department  Em ail: info@StacksBowers.com

Important Information About Bidding, 
Credit and Accepted Forms of Payment 
Below is a summary only. Please review the complete Terms and Conditions of Sale, beginning on page 323, 
or visit www.StacksBowers.com.
Stack’s Bowers Galleries iBid Live
Bid live anywhere in the world, on your computer at StacksBowers.com or use our iOS or Android apps on 
your mobile device or tablet.
Buyer’s Premium and Value Added Tax (V AT)
• For Buyers who take delivery of lots in non-EU countries, a premium of twenty percent (20%) based 
upon the total amount of the hammer (minimum of €25), will be added to all purchases of individual 
lots (the “Buyer’s Premium”). Danish V AT will not apply, provided that the lots in question are exported 
outside of the EU within 30 days from the date of the Auction Sale.
• Buyers which are non-V AT registered entities or individuals who take delivery of lots within EU 
countries or Monaco are subject to margin scheme taxation (in Danish “brugtmoms”) and will be in -
voiced a V AT charge and a Buyer’s Premium for a total of twenty five percent (25%) based upon the total 
amount of the hammer (minimum of €25), which will be added to all purchases of individual lots. For 
Buyers who take delivery of lots within EU countries which are either V AT-exempt or subject to regular 
V AT, a Buyer’s Premium of twenty percent (20%) based upon the total amount of the hammer (minimum 
of €25), will be added (excluding Danish V AT on the hammer plus Buyer’s Premium). 
• Institutional EU Buyers with a valid V AT number registered in an EU member state (other than Den-
mark) will be charged a Buyer’s Premium, but may be exempt from Danish V AT if said EU Buyer pro-
vides the requisite statutory registration documents and can provide documentation that the lot has been 
transported to the foreign address (i.e. outside Denmark) provided by the EU Buyer to the Auctioneer. 
These documents comprise the V AT registration number and the acknowledgement of receipt from the 
territory of the EU member state in which the V AT registration was issued. Should the Auctioneer effect 
the export of any lots, and provided a valid V AT registration document was submitted to the Auctioneer 
before purchase, V AT will not be charged.
Summary of Buyer’s Premium and Value Added Tax (V AT)

========== PAGE 7 ==========

 Pa ge v
Taxable persons are registered for V AT and acquire coins with reference to this registration, e.g. resellers.
Non-taxable persons do not acquire coins with reference to a V AT registration, e.g. collectors.
Lots subject to regular V AT will be marked on the website and in the catalog with the symbol “u” or with the 
text “This item is subject to full V AT” .
Lots which are not subject to V AT will be marked on the website and in the catalog with the symbol “s” or 
with the text “This item is V AT free” .
Placing Bids 
• All bids must be made in Euros. 
• All invoices will be made in Euros.
Bid Limits 
• Bidders without pre-approval by Stack’s Bowers Galleries that plan to bid in excess of their bid limit must 
contact Stack’s Bowers Galleries in advance. A 25% deposit will be required at least 48 hours before the 
start of the auction paid by cashier’s check or money order, approved check, wire transfer or other im-
mediate bank transfer. Any unused deposit will be promptly returned to the bidder. 
Shipping
• All lots will be shipped from Denmark.  Any import (and/or turnover) tax and duty payable abroad is on 
Buyer’s account in every case.
Questions? 
• If you have any questions about bidding, credit or payments, please contact us at 
+1.949.253.0916 / +45.80.40.49.42 or e-mail info@StacksBowers.com / infoDK@StacksBowers.com. 
Accepted Forms of Payment 
• We welcome payment by wire transfer or other immediate bank transfer in Euros.   
• Cash payments ARE NOT accepted. 
• Payment via credit card will not be accepted without prior approval of Stack’s Bowers Galleries.  
All payments by credit card will incur a surcharge of 2.5%. 
• All invoices will be made in Euros.

Always call to verbally confirm wire information to prevent fraud.

Page viii


========== PAGE 11 ==========

 P age ix
It is hard to believe that it has already been over a year since Stack’s Bowers 
Galleries reintroduced L. E. B ruun and his amazing collection of Scandina-
vian numismatic items to the collecting and general publics. Our auctions 
and catalogs have begun to shine a light on his over 20,000 items that have 
been mostly hidden from view, breathing life in full color to items that were 
heretofore listed only as black and white text entries in the 1928 catalog of his core collection. 
With each sale—whether one of the Showcase auctions with an accompanying catalog or the periodic 
Collectors Choice Online auctions of the L.  E. B ruun Collection—we have delved into new corners 
of Bruun’s holdings, slowly expanding his legacy in Scandinavian numismatics and beyond. Top of 
mind in this regard is the incredible offering of British silver Pennies of the Danish King Cnut and his 
successors in this auction. Meticulously selected by lead cataloger Michael Fornitz from among over 
1,000 such Pennies, the quality, rarity, and eye appeal of the 100 pieces in this catalog are uniformly 
high. If you have never purchased a medieval English Penny before, choosing any of the coins in this 
offering will ensure you an amazing coin that will not disappoint. And for those interested in other 
Medieval rarities, fear not, as small but powerful sections of Danish, Norwegian and Swedish medi-
eval coinages are presented in this catalog as well. 
As with all parts of the Bruun Collection, once a coin is sold, there is rarely a duplicate that will ap -
pear in some future sale. So, whether you’re pursuing a marquee rarity like the Danish 1663-dated 4 
Ducats from the Lynge Collection or the modest Swedish Ortug of Erik of Pomerania, devise your 
bidding strategy wisely—you may not have another opportunity for years, if not decades, to acquire a 
comparable item.
Y et another part of the expanding Bruun legacy in this sale is the curated offering of Swedish Plate 
Money. It is hard not to be impressed by the idea—and heft—of copper bullion as money, a unique 
expedient begun by the Swedes in the mid-17th century to turn the abundant copper from Sweden’s 
mines into money. Even the lowest ½ Daler denomination barely fits in your hand, while the extreme-
ly rare 8 Daler is the size of a small TV and at nearly 15 kilos, is comparable to a toddler in weight. 
Bruun’s 8 Daler Plate Money is considered the largest obtainable circulating money in the world, as 
only one example of the larger 10 Daler Plate is known in private hands. 
I would be remiss not to mention that appearing in a separate catalog as a portion of Part III of the L. 
E. Bruun Collection is his small but impressive collection of Scandinavian and related paper curren-
cies. Though Bruun was not a paper money collector per se, his 1922 acquisition of the Bille-Brahe 
collection brought over 300 pieces of paper into his holdings.
To add a final comment about the expansion of the Bruun legacy, we at Stack’s Bowers Galleries have 
been thrilled to play our part. Recently, the catalog for Part I of this legendary collection was bestowed 
the honor of being named the Best World Coin Auction Catalog for the 2025 award year by the Numis-
matic Literary Guild. While it certainly has been a labor to compile all the details and set the context 
which has brought these coins back to life, it is our hope that the love shown by our talented catalogers 
for the collection will continue to inspire a new age of enthusiasts.
To that end, I very much look forward to our return to Copenhagen for this auction—after our L.  E.
Bruun Part II auction in Zurich—bringing the coins and auction back to the place that L. E. Bruun 
called home. If you plan to attend the sale in Copenhagen, be sure to introduce yourself to me and my 
colleagues from our local Copenhagen office.
Matt Orsini
Vice President of World & Ancient Numismatics, Stack’s Bowers Galleries
Welcome Back to the L. E. B ruun Collection: 
A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History
Part III


========== PAGE 12 ==========

Page x
Preface
Although it has been a whole year since I brought down the gavel on the first 
Bruun coin in the lovely ballroom of the Odd Fellow Palace, I feel that the subse-
quent time has simply flown by. I am however quite convinced that many collec-
tors who have anxiously awaited the next sale, do not share this feeling. Still, one 
thing is certain, we are all happy to be back in Copenhagen, very close to where 
L. E. Bruun had his office.
The task of cataloging the Bruun’s vast collection is in many ways akin to a con-
stant treasure hunt. Although the real treasure can admittedly be said to be the collection itself, the 
devil is in the details. For this reason, each and every one of the more than 20,000 pieces must be care-
fully studied, and what at first glance may seem like a relatively common coin often turns out to be an 
extremely rare variant. It is also worth considering that in the 100 years that have passed since Bruun’s 
death, several significant numismatic publications, both Danish and foreign, have been published. Since 
science is always on the move (most of the time even in a forward direction), attributions as to mints, 
moneyers and even sovereigns often move along with it. 
In the present catalog, it is my particular pleasure to present two suites of coins of extraordinary histori-
cal interest, namely the beautiful pennies minted for the Danish kings in England during the first half 
of the 11th century and the Norwegian medieval coins issued before the beginning of the 16th century. 
While the former material has for a long time aroused the interest of international collectors, the Nor-
wegian medieval coins in Bruun’s collection constitute a quite unique opportunity for collectors. As op-
posed to medieval coins from most other European countries, no Norwegian coins minted before 1537 
are allowed to leave the country unless an export permit from the Museum of Cultural History has been 
obtained which is rarely unchallenging. On the rare occasions when such coins appear at Norwegian 
auctions foreign buyers are, as a consequence, consistently excluded from participating. Therefore, if you 
have ever had even the slightest notion that it might be fun to supplement your collection with a few se-
lect Norwegian medieval coins, this is the time to act, because not only does Bruun’s collection (as usual) 
contain some of the best preserved specimens and the rarest types, but they are also freely tradable on 
the market. 
The term “Top Pop” has become a fixed term in today’s world of coin collecting. It simply means that the 
coin in question is at the top (alone or shared) of the total population of similar previously graded coins. 
However, when I have almost omitted to use this expression in the printed Bruun catalogs, it is not only 
because it has a rather frivolous ring to it, but more so because it would be an unbearable repetition of the 
obvious, since it is far more often the rule than the exception that Bruun’s copy is better than the others 
on the market. Furthermore, the individual types and variants are often so rare that no other copies have 
been graded at all, resulting in a mundane VF-25 easily obtaining the Top Pop designation.
The fact that international interest in Bruun’s collection is huge no longer surprises anyone, and the 
spring auction, this time held in Zurich, once again set a number of new price records. A few lament that 
it is no longer possible to keep all these magnificent coins for the nation, but personally I am only pleased 
that so many pieces from Bruun’s collection are being sent out into the world to act as exquisite ambas-
sadors for Scandinavian numismatics, art and history. The national collections in Scandinavia already 
possess incomparable collections, and it can rightly be argued that these auctions alone have let Bruun’s 
coins emerge from the grave to be enjoyed, studied and loved once again. 
I encourage everyone with the slightest interest in numismatics and history to treat themselves to partici-
pating in the Copenhagen viewing, regardless of whether you collect coins, because the chance to hold 
such treasures in your hands is an opportunity that is unlikely to come again. Who knows, maybe it is 
not yet too late to become a collector.
Michael Fornitz
Director of Stack’s Bowers Galleries Denmark


========== PAGE 13 ==========

 P age xi
Forord
Der er gået et helt år, siden jeg slog det første hammerslag i Odd Fellow 
Palæets smukke sal, men skønt den efterfølgende tid for mig er fløjet afsted, 
er jeg sikker på, at der er flere samlere for hvem ventetiden har føltes lang. En 
ting er dog sikkert, vi har alle glædet os til at vende tilbage til København, ikke 
langt fra hvor L. E. Bruun havde sit kontor.
Arbejdet med at katalogisere Bruuns samling er en konstant skattejagt, og 
selvom den egentlige skat naturligvis kan siges at være selve samlingen, ligger 
djævlen som bekendt i detaljen og hver eneste af de mere end 20.000 stykker må derfor nøje stud-
eres, for hvad der ved første øjekast kan synes som en relativ almindelig mønt viser sig ofte at være 
en uhyre sjælden variant. Det er her også værd at betænke, at der i de hundrede år, der er forløbet 
siden Bruuns død, er fremkommet adskillige væsentlige både danske og udenlandske publikationer 
indenfor det numismatiske område, som samlingen vedrører. Videnskaben flytter sig altid, og både 
møntsteder og møntherrer kan flytte med.
I det foreliggende katalog er det mig en særlig glæde for første gang at kunne præsentere to suiter af 
mønter af en ekstraordinær historisk interesse, nemlig de smukke pennies slået for de danske konger 
i England i første halvdel af 1000-tallet, og de norske middelaldermønter præget før 1500-tallet. 
Mens de første altid har vidst at påkalde sig international interesse, udgør de norske middelalder -
mønter i Bruuns samling en  ganske enestående mulighed for samlerne. I modsætning til middel-
aldermønter fra de fleste andre europæiske lande forholder det sig nemlig sådan, at ingen norske 
mønter præget før 1537 må forlade landet, med mindre der foreligger en (vanskeligt opnåelig) ud-
førselstilladelse fra Kulturhistorisk Museum. Af samme årsag udelukkes udenlandske købere kon-
sekvent fra at deltage, når sådanne mønter en sjælden gang optræder på norske auktioner. Har man 
derfor blot den mindste idé om, at det kunne være morsomt at supplere sin samling med nogle 
norske middelaldermønter, gælder det om at kende sin besøgelsestid, for ikke blot rummer Bruuns 
samling som sædvanlig nogle af de ypperst bevarede eksemplarer og de sjældneste typer, men de er 
også frit omsættelige i markedet.
Udtrykket ”Top Pop” , der ganske enkelt betyder, at den pågældende mønt ligger i toppen (alene eller 
delt) af den samlede population af tilsvarende hidtil graderede mønter, er i dagens samlerverden 
blevet et fast begreb. Når jeg imidlertid har undladt at anvende dette udtryk i de trykte Bruunkata-
loger, er det ikke blot fordi, det har en ”useriøs” klang, men i højere grad fordi der ville blive tale om 
en ulidelig gentagelse af det evidente, idet det langt hyppigere er reglen end undtagelsen, at Bruuns 
eksemplar er bedre end de øvrige på markedet. Endvidere at de enkelte typer og varianter ofte så 
sjældne, at der slet ikke er blevet graderet andre eksemplarer, hvorfor selv en halvkedelig VF-25 sag-
tens kan være Top Pop.
At den internationale interesse for Bruuns samling er stor kommer ikke længere bag på nogen, og 
forårets auktion i Zürich satte atter adskillige rekorder. Nogle få begræder, at det ikke længere er mu-
ligt at bevare alle disse mønter i landet, men personligt ser jeg med stor tilfredshed på, at så mange 
eksemplarer fra Bruuns samling sendes ud i verden som udsøgte ambassadører for skandinavisk 
numismatik, kunst og historie. De nationale samlinger i Skandinavien besidder allerede uforligneli-
ge samlinger, og man kan med rette hævde, at først med disse auktioner opstår Bruuns mønter af 
graven og lader sig atter nyde, studere og elske. 
Jeg kan derfor kun opfordre alle med den mindste smule interesse for numismatik og historie til at 
unde sig selv at deltage i eftersynet, uagtet at man ikke samler på mønter, for chancen for at holde så-
danne klenodier i hånden er en chance, der næppe kommer igen, og hvem ved, måske er det endnu 
ikke for sent at blive samler.
Michael Fornitz
Direktør for Stack’s Bowers Galleries Danmark


========== PAGE 14 ==========

Page xii
Wholesale price list from 1896 of L. E. B ruun’s canned Danish Butter, targeting the British market.


========== PAGE 15 ==========

 P age xiii
The Evolution of the L. E. B ruun Collection
Many of you have undoubtedly encountered some version of the joke “The best way to make a small 
fortune in the stock market is to start with a large one. ” Well if we can call L. E. Bruun’s current 20,000 
piece collection of coins, medals and banknotes “small, ” then we can alter the joke to “The best way 
to assemble a small collection of coins is to start with a large one. ”  And in some ways, this is what  
L. E. Bruun did over his nearly 65 years of collecting. However, rather than cleaving off large sections 
of a gargantuan collection, the trajectory of Bruun’s collection as it stands now was a much more 
curated and organic process. 
In an interview on the occasion of his 70th birthday published in the 
March 17, 1922 issue of Danish weekly Hver 8 Dag, Bruun related that 
the origins of his collection extend back to 1859, when an uncle had 
passed away, leaving some coins that were distributed among the chil-
dren in the family. Bruun was about seven years old at the time, an age 
when many children collect something as a means of learning about 
their environment. For most, this is a passing phase in childhood de-
velopment. For a precious few, it is just the beginning, with collect -
ing becoming a lifelong “unruly passion, ” as one author has styled it. 
Bruun was among those precious few. 
Even as a young apprentice in Holbӕk with no disposable income, he 
managed to collect coins, documenting 49 silver coins and 87 coppers 
from around the world in an 1867 listing of his collection, when he 
was just 15 years old. His collecting continued and blossomed in the 
1880s when his butter wholesale and export business took off; this became the inflection point in his 
financial life that provided the resources to buy not only individual coins but even entire collections 
of them. 
He originally and naturally focused on the coins of Denmark and Norway, quickly building up what 
he considered the largest collections in these fields, buying both privately and at public auctions such 
as the sales of the Alfred Benzon collection in Copenhagen in the 1880s. A more complete view of 
the sources, both public and private, for the Scandinavian coins in the Bruun Collection may be seen 
in the list of “Important Auctions and Private Purchases” , provided as an appendix to this auction 
catalog. The listing is a veritable who’s who of Scandinavian numismatics during not only Bruun’s 
lifetime but also the century before. 
He added Swe dish coins to the mix and then English coins in the 1890s, buying at many of the 
famous name sales in England: Montagu, Murdoch, Hilton, Price, Rashleigh, Roth, Walters, and 
Carlyon-Britton. 
The butter business, along with his shrewd investments in real estate on the outskirts of Copen -
hagen, provided the means to collect coins at a high level. The coins provided an escape from the 
day-to-day of the business world, as he related in the Hver 8 Dag interview: “The good thing about 
collecting coins is that when you are upset about something or you feel unsettled, then you go and 
look at your coins, and then you calm down by studying them again and again pondering the many 
unsolved problems they present. People who are exclusively devoted to their business make a great 
mistake. I, for one, could never imagine thinking about nothing but butter until my dying days. ”
His rise to prominence in the field of Danish numismatics landed him an invitation to become a 
founding member of the Danish Numismatic Society in 1885, which undoubtedly provided op -
portunities to rub shoulders with the prominent Danish numismatists of the day and acquire coins 
privately. The pace and extent of his collecting must have been such that he felt the need to sell and 
trade coins during his lifetime, and he is known to have traded items privately within the numismat-
ic milieu of fin-de-siècle Copenhagen. He also auctioned off more than 1,000 lots of coins through 
L. E. Bruun in 1922, around 
the time of his 70th birthday. 

========== PAGE 16 ==========

Page xiv
a general auction in 1894, a named single-owner sale in 1898, and an anonymous single-owner sale 
in 1901.
He was convinced to start auctioning off his Swedish collection, which he felt was the best in pri-
vate hands, a decision he came to regret. The first auction was through Adolph Hess in Frankfurt 
in May 1914, and a second auction was planned for October 1914, but those plans were scuttled by 
the outbreak of World War I. The coins intended for the second auction were instead sold to Swed-
ish merchant and big-time collector Israël Berghman (1864-1945), who sold off a few duplicates at 
auction in 1918. Berghman sold the remainder of the Bruun collection en bloc to Amsterdam dealer 
Jacques Schulman, who in turn resold the collection intact to famed American beer brewer and col-
lector extraordinaire Virgil Brand. 
Bruun’s two biggest coups came late in his life. In 1918, he purchased 
the entire coin collection of J.G. Guildal, who had himself swallowed up 
the entire collection of Major General Carl Thorvald Jørgensen (1819-
1902) in 1901. Bruun’s crowning achievement, though, was the 1922 
acquisition of the multigenerational Countship of Brahesminde Collec-
tion, built by the Bille-Brahe clan commencing circa 1800. Additional 
details on these important collectors and collections can be found in 
this catalog on page 93 (Guildal) and page 31 (Brahesminde). 
Bruun’s collections were already quite advanced at the time of these two 
important acquisitions, which resulted in a fair amount of duplication 
in the resulting blended collection. Bruun himself sold off some of the 
Guildal duplicates to prominent Danish collectors, but it wasn’t until af-
ter his passing that the Bille-Brahe collection was fully integrated into 
Bruun’s. The duplicates, selected by Danish numismatists Georg Galster 
and Hans Henrik Schou, were sold at auction under the auspices of Hol-
ger Hede in a 6,241 lot auction in October 1925. Also in 1925 were a pair 
of auctions by Sotheby’s in London that dispersed his British, Continental and American collec-
tions, while over 500 medieval pennies of Northumberland, Ireland, as well as Nordic imitations of 
Aethelred’s coins were donated to the Danish Royal Collection of Coins and Medals by a provision 
of Bruun’s will.
Given its depth and size, the Bille-Brahe collection was long considered a reserve for the Danish 
National Collection, a mantle that passed to Bruun with the acquisition of the Bille-Brahe cabinet. 
As a patriotic Dane and the most prominent collector of his day, Bruun took seriously the idea that 
his collection was now an informal reserve for the Danish National Collection. Though Copenhagen 
and all Denmark were spared the destruction suffered by much of Europe during World War I, he 
was keenly aware that Copenhagen had been mostly destroyed by British bombardment in 1807, and 
that a future world conflagration could result in the loss or destruction of the National Collection. 
In 1922 he amended his will to formalize the concept of his collection acting as a reserve for the Na-
tional Collection: “For a period of 100 years after my death, the collection shall serve as a reserve for 
the Royal Coin and Medal Collection in Copenhagen in the sense that if it should be substantially 
destroyed by fire or lost by theft or other such event, my collection shall be offered to the State as a 
gift. ” However, should the next century pass with the national collection intact, “it shall be sold at 
public auction and the proceeds shall accrue to the persons who are my direct descendants. ”
On November 21, 2023, exactly 100 years after Bruun’s death, the waiting period ran out. With the 
Royal Danish Coin and Medal Collection intact, L. E. Bruun’s fantastic 20,000-piece collection of 
Danish, Norwegian and Swedish numismatics built and curated through decades of acquisitions and 
conscious whittling, became destined for the auction block. The fruits of nearly 200 years of labor of 
generations of collector-curators, culminating with L. E. Bruun, will re-enter the numismatic mar-
ketplace through a series of Stack’s Bowers Galleries auctions over the coming years.
The catalog of the  
L. E. Bruun Collection  
duplicates sold at auction 
in Copenhagen in  
October 1925.

========== PAGE 20 ==========

Page 2 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
THE ANGLIAN COINS OF CNUT, HAROLD AND HARTHACNUT
COINS OF GREAT BRITAIN
King Cnut, also known as Canute with the epithet “the Great” , is widely recog-
nized as one of the greatest of all Danish kings. He was born around 990 as the 
son of Sweyn Forkbeard and was chosen by the Vikings and the people of the 
Danelaw to become the new king of England following the death of his father 
in 1014. This did, however, not go down well with the English nobility who pe-
titioned Æthelred to return from his exile in Normandy. Cnut was forced to flee 
to Denmark but returned with a vengeance as the leader of an invading force of 
10,000 Vikings aboard 200 longships and in 1016 Cnut was hailed as king of all 
England. Following the death of his older brother Harald in 1018, he ascended 
the throne of Denmark, and from 1028 he also ruled Norway, parts of Sweden 
and Pomerania, completing the so-called North Sea Empire. 
When Cnut died in 1035, his son by Emma of Normandy, Harthacnut, as the 
legitimate heir, took over the throne in both Denmark and England, but as 
fighting in the Scandinavian region prevented him from going to England, 
the English throne soon lay open for his half-brother, Harold, with the epithet 
Harefoot, who already died in 1040. Harthacnut soon returned and took hold 
of the kingdom peacefully. For two years until the death of Harthacnut on 
June 8th, 1042, England and the Norse Empire was for the last time united under a Danish king.
Although often seen as a Viking ruler, Cnut did in fact protect England from rogue Viking hordes and the economy 
flourished during his relatively peaceful reign of almost 20 years. Cnut simply took over many forms of Anglo-Saxon 
government, including a successful coinage. Rather than at a single royal mint, coins were produced in towns all over 
England, making it quite easy to obtain new coins in all areas.  
Almost all of the busts that appear on earlier Anglo-Saxon coins are fairly crude copies of Roman coin designs of the 
late third to early fifth centuries, and Cnut was the first English king to introduce a more contemporary style of bust. 
Whereas his first type, the so-called “Quatrefoil” type, minted from circa 1016-1023, shows him in a crown similar 
to those pictured in Anglo-Saxon manuscripts of the same period, his second type from circa 1023-1030 shows him 
wearing a pointed helmet. This type of helmet was typical of those worn by Anglo-Saxons, Normans and Vikings in 
the eleventh century. Cnut’s last type referred to as “short cross” , issued between 1029 and 1035 bears a more tradi-
tional “Romanesque” portrait of the draped king wearing a diadem. These three types were a fixed standard for all 
Mints although there were distinct variations in stylistic details, making it possible to speak of for instance, a Lincoln 
or a Winchester style. Weights consistently vary around 1 gm.
Both Harold and Harthacnut basically only minted two different types. The first, which was minted during the 
shared Regency period which lasted from Cnut’s death until 1037, is named the Jewel Cross type after the central 
ornamentation of the reverse comprising of four ovals, combined at their bases, enclosing a pellet.
The second type of Harold struck during his sole reign from 1038-1040 is called Fleur-de-Lis after the four stylized 
lilies in the angles of the reverse voided cross.
The Second type of Harthacnut, the so-called “arm and sceptre type” dated 
between 1040 and 1042 was sometimes struck in the name of Cnut, and not 
surprisingly this “sub-type” (BMC XVII, Hildebrand I) was previously (in -
cluding the time of the 1928, Bruun catalog) believed to be minted for Cnut 
himself. 
The weight and the silver content of the Pennies struck by Harold and Har -
thacnut roughly corresponds to the standard set by their father and in many 
or most cases the moneyers were the same.
King Cnut "the Great" illustrated
in an initial of a medieval manuscript.
Mortuary chest from Winchester Cathedral 
in England containing the bones of 
King Cnut and his wife Emma.

rare, the present one with the archaic, diademed portrait (Hildebrand type Ed) immensely so.

11021
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1029-36). Derby Mint. Cnut. NGC AU-58. Short Cross (BMC xvi) type; S-1159; 
North-790; Hild-277; SCBI Cop-309 (plate coin); Bruun-87. Weight: 1.01 gms. Moneyer: Swertinc. The name of Derby 
originates from the Viking name Djúra Bý (“Village of the Deer”) and was originally one of the five fortified towns of the 
Danelaw. Excessively rare Mint for this type with the present example being both handsomely toned and very detailed.
€2,000-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

11027
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1029-36). Y ork Mint. Cnut. 
NGC MS-64. Short Cross (BMC xvi) type; S-1159; North-790; 
Hild-482; SCBI Cop-545 (plate coin); Bruun-205. Weight: 1.15 
gms. Moneyer: Beorn. Nice original luster and fine details.
Beorn (Norse for “Bear”) continued to produce coins at Y ork 
right up until the Norman conquest in 1066.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 15
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Legendary “Castle Gotha” Penny in Lovely Condition
11031
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1040-42). Gothaburh Mint. Harthacnut. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Arm 
and Sceptre (BMC xvii) type in the name of Cnut; S-1169; North-799; cf. Hild-1000; SCBI Cop(Cnut)-1061 (plate 
coin); Bruun-272. Weight: 1.10 gms. Moneyer: Leomær. Extremely rare and interesting Mint. A gorgeous Penny with a 
sublime toning, exquisite details and a nice even flan.
The location of the very rare mint “Gothaburh, ” also cited as “Castle Gotha, ” remains uncertain. In the 1928 Bruun catalog, 
Galster points to Ythanceaster in Essex, but stylistic details have led Dolley and Elmore-Jones to suggest a location in West 
Wessex.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11032
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1029-36). Cambridge Mint. Cnut. NGC MS-63. Short Cross (BMC xvi) type; S-1159; 
North-790; Hild-1034; SCBI Cop-1083 (plate coin); Bruun-281. Weight: 1.07 gms. Moneyer: Edwine. Scarce, attrac-
tively toned and with a bold strike. 
€700-€900
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Wonderful Langport Rarity
11040
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1024-30). Langport Mint. Cnut. NGC MS-62. Pointed Helmet (BMC xiv) type; 
S-1158; North-787; Hild - (type G); SCBI Cop-1296 (plate coin); Bruun-337. Weight: 0.88 gms. Moneyer: Edric. Sharply 
struck with very clear legends and a fine dark patina. With only the present coin in the Bruun collection all issues from 
Langport are extremely rare with Pointed helmet types generally considered unobtainable. The moneyer, Edric, is not 
recorded by Hildebrand for this Mint..
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11039

North-787; Hild-1264; SCBI Cop-1308 (plate coin); Bruun-331. Weight: 0.97 gms. Moneyer: Eadwine. Near Gem with 
wonderful luster and unusually fine details in the helmet. 
€600-€800
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1029-36). Lympne Mint. Cnut.

Hild-1461; SCBI Cop-1502 (plate coin); Bruun-376. Weight: 
1.08 gms. Moneyer: Godric. The small Kentish mint at Lympne 
was disbanded after the reign of Cnut. All types from the reign 
of Cnut are excessively rare and the Short Cross type practically 
never appear on the market.

Outstanding Stafford Rarity
11061
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1016-23). Stafford Mint. Cnut. NGC AU-58. Quatrefoil (BMC viii) type; S-1157; 
North-781; cf. Hild - (type E); SCBI Cop-3490 (plate coin); Bruun-847. Weight: 1.14 gms. Moneyer: Ælfric. Distinctly 
engraved and sharply struck with a fine, old cabinet toning. Coins from the reign of Cnut minted at Stafford in the West 
Midlands belong among the legendary rarities with not a single example of the Quatrefoil type being listed in Hildeb-
rand.
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

in the name of Cnut; S-1169; North-799; Hild- (type I); SCBI 
Cop(Cnut)-3798 (plate coin); Bruun-958. Weight: 1.07 gms. 
Moneyer: Brunstan. Fine old cabinet toning. Very rare. 
€1,400-€1,800
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 44 ==========

Page 26 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Near-Choice Warwick Quatrefoil Penny
11068
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1016-23). Warwick Mint. Cnut. NGC MS-62. Quatrefoil (BMC viii) type; S-1157; 
North-781; Hild - (type E); SCBI Cop-3912 (plate coin); Bruun-964. Weight: 1.16 gms. Moneyer: Wulfric. A handsome, 
detailed strike with a fine, light cabinet toning. Excessively rare mint for Cnut no matter the type with Quatrefoil Pen-
nies being of the utmost rarity. Wulfric is the only Warwick moneyer known for any preceding type.
During the Danish invasion of Mercia in 1016 much of the city was destroyed but Cnut continued to use the mint that was 
originally established during the reign of Æthelstan.
€1,500-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11067
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1024-30). Totnes Mint. Cnut. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Pointed Helmet (BMC 
xiv) type; S-1158; North-787; Hild-3566 (Totleigh); SCBI Cop-4222 (plate coin); Bruun-962. Weight: 1.03 gms. Moneyer: 
Wulfwerd. Nicely struck and exquisitely toned. All coins struck for Cnut at the Devon mint of Totnes are extremely rare. 
Totnes is said to be the place where the mythical founder of Britain, Brutus of Troy first came ashore. 
€1,200-€1,600
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 45 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 27
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Gorgeous and Exceedingly Rare Watchet Penny
11069
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1024-30). Watchet Mint. Cnut. NGC MS-62. Pointed Helmet (BMC xiv) type; S-1158; 
North-787; Hild - (type G); SCBI Cop-3919 (plate coin); Blackburn-23b (this coin); Bruun-969. Weight: 1.12 gms. 
Moneyer: Gotcild. Superbly struck, lustrous and with a gorgeous multicolored toning. Excessively rare. For a period of 
35 years Godcild seems to have been the only moneyer at the Somerset town of Watchet and the present coin is struck 
from the only known Pointed Helmet dies from this mint.
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11070
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1029-36). Wallingford Mint. Cnut. NGC MS-65. Short Cross (BMC xvi) type; S-1159; 
North-790; cf. Hild-3597; SCBI Cop-3929 (plate coin); Bruun-975. Weight: 1.14 gms. Moneyer: Ælfwine. A light crack in 
the obverse surface does not deter from the fact that this is indeed a superlative Gem Mint State Penny with a splendid 
toning. This issue depicts an interesting and highly irregular obverse inscription reading: CNVCNVT (Hild, ir. 10). 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Page 28 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Impossibly Rare Wareham Penny
11071
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1016-23). Wareham Mint. Cnut. NGC AU Details—Peck Marked. Quatrefoil (BMC 
viii) type; S-1157; North-781; Hild - (type E); SCBI Cop-3949 (plate coin); Bruun-978. Weight: 0.99 gms. Moneyer: 
Oda. Clear legends and a fine, gunmetal grey patina. Coins from the reign of Cnut struck at the mint of Wareham 
(Werham) are impossibly rare even in public collections with none in the National Museum of Denmark and only one 
(a Short Cross type) recorded by Hildebrand in the Royal Swedish Collection.
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11072
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1024-30). Worcester Mint. Cnut. NGC MS-65. Pointed Helmet (BMC xiv) type; 
S-1158; North-787; Hild-3627; SCBI Cop-3952 (plate coin); Bruun-981. Weight: 1.11 gms. Moneyer: Ægelwine. An 
absolutely stunning survivor from the late Viking age utterly deserving the MS65 designation. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

11075
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1016-23). Winchester Mint. Cnut. NGC MS-64. Quatrefoil (BMC viii) type; S-1157; 
North-781; Hild-3727; SCBI Cop-4068 (plate coin); Bruun-1000. Weight: 0.91 gms. Moneyer: Cunna. A wonderful 
example with a perfect even strike, soft luster and a fine gun-metal grey toning. 
The Hampshire city of Winchester was one of the most important mints during the reign of Cnut, whose mortuary chest 
can still be seen in Winchester Cathedral. 
€800-€1,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ravishing Arm and Sceptre Penny
11076
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1040-42). Winchester Mint. Harthacnut. NGC MS-62. Arm and Sceptre (BMC xvii) 
type in the name of Cnut; S-1169; North-799; Hild-3744; SCBI Cop(Cnut)-4083 (plate coin); Bruun-1051. Weight: 
1.12 gms. Moneyer: Godman. Peckmark in reverse fourth quarter. Stunningly lustrous and well-struck from a nicely 
executed die. Very rare.
Like his father, Harthacnut also found his final resting place in Winchester Cathedral. 
€1,800-€2,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Harold I Harefoot. NGC MS-62. Fleur de Lis (BMC Vc) type;

toning.

catalog attributes it to Hereford, there can be little doubt that the 
issue was actually struck at the Hertfordshire county town.

COINS OF DENMARK
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11105
DENMARK. Semi-Bracteate, ND (975-980). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Harald Blåtand (Bluetooth). NGC MS-63. 
Hauberg-1; Malmer KG 9c (CE III:C4, Pl. 14:10); Bruun-1236 (illustrated plate 2). Weight: 0.26 gms. Superlatively struck 
and with a most enchanting toning with speckles of purple and turquoise.
Only the side imitating Charlemagne’s type from Dorestad has been struck making the present semi-bracteate a “regular” 
bracteate and very rare thus. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Excessively Rare and Interesting Semi-Bracteate
11106
DENMARK. Semi-Bracteate, ND (c. 985). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Harald Blåtand (Bluetooth). NGC MS-62. Cf. 
Hauberg-6; Malmer-KG 10c (K:C, Pl.32:19 / K:A1a, Pl. 31:1-2); Hauberg Collection -; Bruun-1302 (illustrated plate 2). 
Weight: 0.17 gms. Sharply struck with a fine old, cabinet toning. Small edge split. An excessively rare very late double 
cross type, with only three examples of this variant recorded by Malmer, one in the National Museum of Copenhagen 
and one in Stockholm.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 61 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 43
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Handsome and Intriguing Cnut Penny
11107
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1018-35). Lund Mint. Cnut (Knud den Store). NGC AU Details—Peck Marked. Hau -
berg-3; Hauberg Collection -; Malmer-9.738.1917; Cf. Hild-3780 note; Bruun-1313. Weight: 1.36 gms. Moneyer: Leof-
noth. Nicely struck on a broad, even planchet.
Highly interesting and extremely rare mule between a possibly original Winchester reverse die and a Lund obverse with 
the king’s Danish title (REX DÆNORVM)
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Photo Enlarged 2x
11108
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1018-35). Viborg Mint. Cnut (Knud 
den Store). NGC MS-63. Hauberg-49; Hauberg Collection-69; 
Bruun-1343. Weight: 0.71 gms. Moneyer: unknown (blundered 
legends). A truly remarkable survivor with much of the original 
luster preserved, a lovely, delicate toning and an excellent strike.
As opposed to the English types minted for Æthelræd II, the Manus 
Dei (“Hand of God”) extending down from the sky is placed on the 
obverse.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

11113
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1035-42). Aalborg Mint. Harthacnut (Hardeknud). NGC MS-65. Hauberg-39; Hauberg 
Collection-572; Galster, Kongsø-26; Bruun-1481. Weight: 0.78 gms. Moneyer: Unknown (blundered legends). Gem 
uncirculated and virtually true perfection for issue, very rare thus.
This coin was a part of the important hoard found at Kongsø Plantation (Mid-Jutland) in 1905 and acquired by Bruun in 
an exchange with the Royal Coin Cabinet at the Danish National Museum.

Page 48 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Highly Interesting and Important Penny
11118
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-47). Lund Mint. Magnus I, “the Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC AU Details—Peck 
Marked. Hauberg-13; Hauberg Collection-689; C. J. Becker-M 16/35; Bruun-1607 (illustrated plate 6). Weight: 0.65 
gms. Moneyer: Arcil. Nice, deep toning and fully readable legends on both sides. No more than about a handful of ex-
amples are known of this extremely interesting type. It combines an enigmatic obverse showing a small en face head in 
front of the portrait with a reverse design specific to Ørbæk. The provincial mint of Ørbæk in Northern Jutland seems 
to have been active only during the reigns of Cnut and Harthacnut.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 241.

========== PAGE 67 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 49
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Excessively Rare, Choice and of Great Historical Significance
11121
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1042-47). Haithabu (Hedeby) Mint. Magnus I, “the Good” (Magnus den gode). NGC 
MS-63. Hauberg-37; Hauberg Collection -; Bruun-1630 (illustrated plate 6). Weight: 0.81 gms. Moneyer: Ioli (Jule). 
Superbly struck and boasting a fine luster. Impossibly rare, presumably the only one in private hands and easily one of 
the most outstanding Viking Period / Late Medieval Pennies in the Bruun collection.
The type features a portrait of Magnus’ father, the Norwegian king, Olav Haraldsson (“the Holy”). He is easily recognized 
by his attribute, the so-called Dane Axe, a long two-handed axe which can still be seen in the Norwegian Coat of Arms. It 
was said that it was such an axe that delivered a fatal blow against Olav in the Battle of Stiklestad. Just one-year after his 
death he was canonized by the pope in Rome. 
Professor C. J. Becker has convincingly suggested that the present type should be considered the oldest commemorative coin 
of Denmark, struck by Magnus in the years following his expulsion from the Eastern part of Denmark by Svend Estridsen 
to glorify his victory over the Slavs at Lyrskov Heath, West of Haithabu, in 1043. Legend has it that Olav showed himself in 
Magnus’ dreams the night before the battle helping him to achieve success.
€7,000-€9,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 68 ==========

Page 50 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Lovely and Enigmatic Christian Type
11122
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Estridsen. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-17; 
Hauberg Collection-912; Bruun-1714 (illustrated plate 7). Weight: 0.95 gms. Moneyer: Unknown (blundered legends). 
Arresting details and wonderful, old cabinet toning. A scarce religious type featuring the Lamb of God (nimbate) and 
the Holy Dove in an interesting style with the Dove resembling a Roman legionary eagle. The Agnus Dei is holding a 
Bible between its forelegs and carries an inexplicable figure on its back.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Alfred Benzon Collection (Copenhagen - 1887) Lot # 335.

========== PAGE 69 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 51
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Rare and Fascinating Celtic Inspired Penny
11123
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Estridsen. NGC AU Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-19; 
Hauberg Collection-916; C. J. Becker-143 (reverse); Bruun-1719 (illustrated plate 7). Weight: 0.92 gms. Moneyer: Len-
thu. Almost invisible daylight spots but otherwise a remarkable example of this extremely rare, amusing and highly 
interesting type.
The model for this singular type seems to have been Celtic imitations of the famous Macedonian horseman types struck for 
Philip II. The emissions must have taken place during the turbulent years when Magnus and Svend were fighting for power.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Page 52 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Excessively Rare and Charming Byzantine Type
11124
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Estridsen. NGC Unc Details—Peck Marked. Hauberg-23; 
Hauberg Collection-919; C. J. Becker-129 (reverse); Bruun-1721 (illustrated plate 7). Weight: 0.96 gms. Moneyer: Leisti. 
Charming, dark patina. Excessively rare type struck very early in the reign of Svend. The model seems to be the His -
tamenon Nomisma struck for the Byzantine emperors around 1000 AD.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 72 ==========

Page 54 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Extremely Rare and Amusing “Grumpy Balloons” Penny
11128
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Viborg Mint. Svend II Estridsen. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. Hauberg-63; 
Hauberg Collection-1159; Bruun-1947 (illustrated plate 8). Weight: 0.54 gms. Moneyer: Unknown (blundered legends). 
Although at some point possibly slightly wiped, an absolutely wonderful, lustrous and lightly toned example. Extremely 
rare and highly coveted.
Although this spectacular type, in Denmark affectionately nicknamed the “grumpy balloons”, must have a remote model 
among the emissions from the Byzantine Empire, the unknown moneyer has managed to give these dies a both profoundly 
original and distinctly Nordic expression. The many enigmatic details as the bird at the upper obverse rim and the four 
small faces at the end of the reverse cross further enhances the bewitching charm of this singular type.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

legends). A single peck mark in no way undermines the fact that this is an extraordinarily nice example from this elusive 
mint. Sharply struck and with a fine, old cabinet toning. Extremely rare.

11130

tion-1198; Poulsen-SK1.1a; Bruun-1983. Weight: 0.83 gms. Moneyer: Svafa. Delicately toned mint state example.
The standing figure carrying a crook is nimbate and could be interpreted as an anonymous canonized bishop but might just 
as well be an interpretation of Christ as “the Good Shepherd” (John 10:1-21).

Extremely Rare and Pleasing Cross Staff Type
11131

Interesting type where the figure is turned to his right and holds a cross staff instead of a crook. Although this emission is 
known from the hands of 14 different moneyers, it is excessively rare with only a very few in private hands.

Excessively Rare Bracteate with the Full Name of Canute
11139
DENMARK. Penny (Bracteate), ND (1146-57). Northern Jutland. Canute (Knud) V . NGC—Chipped. Hauberg-4; 
Hauberg Collection -; Poulsen-JY22; Bruun-2250. Weight: 0.18 gms. Lightly chipped. Remarkably detailed and with a 
lovely, dark patina. Extremely rare, early bracteate giving the full name and title of the king: +KANVT REX DAN. 
Following the death of Erik Lamb, Denmark was thrown into a civil war between three pretenders to the throne, all descend-
ing from different Danish kings: Canute, the grandson of Niels, Svend, the son of Erik Emune, and Valdemar, the grandson 
of Erik Ejegod. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

11141
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1157-82). Roskilde Mint. Valdemar I 
(Valdemar d. Store). NGC AU Details—Environmental Dam -
age. Hauberg-6; Hauberg Collection-1463; Poulsen-SJ13.4; Bru-
un-2274 (illustrated plate 10). Weight: 0.85 gms. The so-called 
environmental damage certainly doesn’t prevent this from being 
among the best available. A rare and unusual type and probably 
the first type struck for Valdemar in Roskilde (1157-1158). 
€800-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11142
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1157-82). Roskilde Mint. Valdemar 
I (Valdemar d. Store). NGC AU-55. Hauberg-7; Hauberg Col-
lection-1465; Poulsen-SJ15; Bruun-2275 (illustrated plate 10). 
Weight: 0.76 gms. Very nicely struck and with a fine, old cabinet 
toning.
An extremely rare type presumably struck around 1158-1160 for 
Bishop Absalon, the legendary “founder” of Copenhagen, who is 
here highly unusual depicted in profile. An equestrian statue of 
him, erected in 1901 (on the 700th anniversary of his death) still 
stands on Højbro Plads in Central Copenhagen; the archbishop is 
wearing a battle helmet and brandishing a axe. 
€1,200-€1,800
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11143
DENMARK. Penny (Bracteate), ND (1157-82). Northern 
Jutland. Valdemar I (Valdemar d. Store). NGC AU-55. Hau -
berg-51; cf. Hauberg Collection-1514; Poulsen-JY34.6; Bru -
un-2326. Weight: 0.18 gms. Exceptional example retaining some 
of the original luster. Rare type almost only known from the 
hoard from Biersted (Bjergsted) Heath that was found in 1860. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11144
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1202-41). Roskilde Mint. Valdemar 
II (the Victorious). NGC VF Details—Scratches. Hauberg-30; 
Hauberg Auction 1606; Poulsen-SJ43; Bruun-2406. Weight: 0.88 
gms. Bishop: Niels Stigsen. Excessively rare and historically in-
teresting type where not only the name and title of the king: 
+W ALDEMARVS REX D (Valdemar, king of Denmark) but also 
the name and title of the bishop is given in full: +NICOLAVS 
EPC (episcopus = bishop) clearly demonstrating that the clergy 
had a substantial share in the right of coinage. Traces of double 
striking on the reverse.
Niels Stigsen of the noble Hvide dynasty was elected bishop of 
Roskilde in 1224 and rose to the office of chancellor of the realm 
during Erik Plovpenning. After a serious conflict with the king in 
1245 he was forced to leave the country and he died in the convent 
of Clairvaux in 1249. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 78 ==========

Page 60 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Only a Very Few in Private Hands
11146
DENMARK. Witten, ND (c. 1400). Næstved Mint. Erik VII of Pomerania. NGC EF Details—Environmental Dam-
age. Galster-2; Sieg-11; FL-2; Jesse-694; Bruun-3725 (illustrated plate 34). Weight: 1.09 gms. In spite of the inevitable 
and natural find patina, a most presentable example with clear legends. Excessively rare and historically very important 
issue where the obverse (ERICVS REX D S N) for the first time proclaims that the Danish king is now also king of 
Sweden and Norway. Of the four examples in the Stege Hoard found in 1898, two are in National Museum, one is in 
Museum Odense and one (the present) in the Bruun Collection.
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex. Stege Hoard, 1898.
    
11145
DENMARK. Søsling (Sechsling), ND (after 1424). Lund Mint. Erik VII of Pomerania. NGC EF-40. Galster-11; Sieg-
13.1; FL-59; Jesse-484; Bruun-3712. Weight: 1.37 gms. Moneyer: Goswin Comhaer (?). Unusually nice and clearly struck 
example of this rare type, known as “Queen Philippa’s Søsling” . It was issued in accordance with the Hanseatic decree of 
October 8th 1424.
The Latin title, “DEI GRATIA ” appears here for the first time on a Danish coin. Either in full or in an abbreviated form, it 
became a standard part of all Danish coin legends up until the late 19th century, indicating that the king was appointed “by 
the grace of God”. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Christian Jürgensen Thomsen Collection (Copenhagen - 1876) Lot # 11084.

========== PAGE 79 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 61
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
11147
DENMARK. Sterling, ND (1440-48). Malmö Mint. Christopher III of Bavaria. NGC AU-53. Galster-19a; Sieg-1.1; 
FL-17; Jesse-704; Bruun-3757. Weight: 0.65 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Myndel. Charming and wonderfully detailed exam-
ple of this rare type minted at the newly established Mint at the fortress Malmøhus, built for Erik of Pomerania in 1434. 
€2,000-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11148
DENMARK. Skilling, ND (1440-42). Lund Mint. Christopher III of Bavaria. NGC EF-45. Galster-18; Sieg-4; FL-11; 
Jesse-705; Bruun-3763. Weight: 1.87 gms. Unusually attractive example of the first Danish Skilling. This denomination 
wasn’t discontinued until 1874.
The reverse legend: “GLORIA IN EXCELSIS DEO” was taken from the Song of Angels (Luke 2:14) 
€800-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
11149
DENMARK. Skilling, ND (1448). Malmö Mint. Privy Council. NGC EF-40. Galster-21; Sieg-1; Schou-4; Bruun-3777. 
Weight: 2.10 gms. With this very rare type being almost impossible to find in a decent condition, this highly detailed 
example truly stands out.
Struck by the Privy Council during the Interregnum from the death of Christopher III of Bavaria on January 6th, 1448, and 
the election of Christian (I) on September 28th the same year.
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11150
DENMARK. Skilling, 1512. Malmö Mint. Hans. NGC EF-45. Galster-29; Sieg-6; Schou-10; Bruun-3837. Weight: 2.38 
gms. Mintmaster: Hans Sever. A sensationally lustrous example with clear legends and a perfect flan. Apart from the 
‘ Anno Domino 1234’ Penny and the Hans gold Nobles this is the earliest dated Danish coin, and the only one obtainable 
for the average collector. 
€600-€900
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 80 ==========

Page 62 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
The Bille-Brahe family, one of the most important noble families in the his -
tory of Denmark, can be traced to the Middle Ages. The family name arose 
from a merger of the two noble families Bille and Brahe. There have been 
many prominent members of the family over the years, but the most famous 
by far is world-renowned astronomer Tycho Brahe (1546-1601), son of Otto 
Brahe and Beate Clausdatter Bille. 
The most significant collector in the Bille-Brahe family was Count Preben 
Bille-Brahe (1773-1857), who is seen here with his second wife, Johanne Car-
oline Wilhelmine b. Falbe.
They were portrayed in 1817 by C.W . Eckersberg, one of Denmark’s most tal-
ented painters from the time art historians refer to as Denmark’s golden age. It 
shows a tanned man who was out on the farm during the day and in the eve-
ning sat with a book in his hand, together with his spouse, who, of course, was 
occupied with her needlework. The painting can be seen at the Ny Carlsberg 
Glyptotek in Copenhagen.
As a young man, Preben Bille-Brahe trained as an officer in the horse guard, 
after which (as the times dictated), he set off on a Grand Tour, which led him 
to Italy. It was there his great interest in art and history, so exquisitely reflected in his coin collection, was truly awak-
ened. He also visited the British Isles to study agriculture to benefit the agricultural exploitation of the family estate, 
Hvedholm. In 1798, the Countship of Brahesminde was established by uniting the lands from Hvedholm, Damsbo, 
Stensgård and Østrupgård as well as the two islands Avernakø and Drejø. Preben Bille-Brahe was considered both a 
skilled and humane estate administrator, particularly active in improving the living conditions of small-scale work-
ers, village teachers, etc. He not only had humanistic interests, but was also generally interested in science; it is by no 
means accidental that Eckersberg let Bille-Brahe hold a book in his hand. The count’s personal library at Hvedholm 
was eagerly added to, but he also supported the creation of a public library on Funen. In 1840 he received the Grand 
Cross of the Order of Dannebrog and in 1841 he was appointed Privy Councilor to the court. 
His son, diplomat Henrik Bille-Brahe (1798-1875) and grandson, Preben Charles Bille-Brahe Selby (1842-1918) 
continued to increase the collection. But at the disentailing of the estate in 1920, the countship’s collection held in 
Hvedholm Manor had to be disposed of in favor of the Treasury. The collection was valued at DKK 175,000 by Julius 
W . Wilcke and H. H. Schou, who prepared the main 
part of the L. E. Bruun catalog. With the intervention 
of Georg Galster (who was head of the coin cabinet in 
the National Museum, to which L. E. Bruun’s collection 
would be bequeathed in the unlikely event that the col-
lection held here should be destroyed or stolen within 
the next 100 years), it was decided to accept Bruun’s 
bid of DKK 140,000, and the deal was completed on 21 
October, 1922. 
Many of the most sublime pieces in L. E. Bruun’s collec-
tion come from the collection of the Countship of Bra-
hesminde, and there is no doubt that many specimens 
among the 19th century coins went straight from the 
Mint into this collection. The Bille-Brahe family’s close 
relationship with other Danish noble families, where 
exceptional pieces had been treasured for generations, 
enabled them to acquire coins that would otherwise 
not have been available to collectors. 
THE COLLECTION OF
THE COUNTSHIP OF BRAHESMINDE
Count Preben Bille-Brahe with his second 
wife, Johanne Caroline Wilhelmine b. Falbe.
Aerial view of Hvedholm Castle.

One of Denmark’s First Gold Coins
11151
DENMARK. Goldgulden (Rhinsk Gulden), ND (1496-1497). Malmö or Copenhagen Mint. Hans. NGC EF-45. 
Fr-4; Galster-27A; Sieg-10; Schou 2; Bruun-3839. Weight: 3.17 gms. Mintmaster: Herman van Nassau or Hans Sever. 
Tiny crack in the flan but otherwise a most pleasant example of one of Denmark’s first gold coins, struck to finance the 
German mercenaries in the war against the Swedish rebel king, Sten Gustafsson Sture. Rare.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

2.36 gms. Mintmaster: Dines Blicher. Beautifully toned and lustrous, scarce in any condition and very rare thus. 
€800-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 82 ==========

Magnificent Early Dated Taler of the Utmost Rarity
11153
DENMARK. Silver Gulden, 1518. Malmö Mint. Christian II. NGC VF-30. Dav-8220; Galster-38; Sieg-9; Schou-2; 
Bruun-3931. Weight: 28.88 gms. Mintmaster: Dines Blicher. An utterly captivating example with a lovely, old cabinet 
toning. Of the utmost rarity with only a handful of examples known. Struck from dies made for the Gold Noble (Fr-6) 
which is today only known in the Danish National Museum.
The design was copied from the Nobles of king Hans but with a new reverse legend taken from the Liber Sapientiae 9:7: XPS 
IHS ELEGIT ME REGEM POPVLO SVO (“Christ Jesus chose me to govern his people”)
€25,000-€30,000

11154
DENMARK. Skilling / 10 Hvide, 1523. Roskilde or Serridslev Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU-55. Galster-51; Sieg-22.3; 
Jensen & Skjoldager-L-01/L-34; Schou-3; Bruun-3957. Weight: 3.97 gms. Mintmaster: Berndt Buske. A splendid example 
with a remarkable luster and superior details. Very rare variant with date on both sides.
This interesting issue was struck during the siege of Copenhagen at a military camp either in Roskilde or the village of Ser-
ridslev. The reverse bears the Latin inscription: DEO DVCE MILITES TRUMPHANT (‘With God’s leadership the troops will 
be victorious”). 
€1,200-€1,600
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Lauritz C. Petersen Collection (Copenhagen - 1914) Lot # 106.
    
11155
DENMARK. Skilling, 1524. Ribe Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU Details—Environmental Damage. Galster-70; Sieg-21; 
Jensen & Skjoldager-S-01/S-05; Schou-4; Bruun-3964. Weight: 2.17 gms. Exceptionally well-preserved and presumably 
the nicest example in private ownership. This very rare type struck before the coronation is often referred to as a 14 Penny.
Whereas the Danish Arms with the three lions have been chosen for the reverse just like the preceding Skillings from Christian 
II and Hans, the armored figure of the king is shown with the Holsatian nettle between his legs. 
€2,000-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenhagen - 1899) Lot # 138.
    
11156
DENMARK. 14 Penny, 1524. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik I. NGC EF-40. Galster-56; Sieg-18; Jensen & Skjoldager-F-
53/F-57; Schou-5; Bruun-3978. Weight: 1.89 gms. In spite of a small hairline split near the rim, this is still a vastly superior 
example of a very rare type.
The half moon and the star flanking the obverse shield have been part of Copenhagen’s coat of arms since the 13th century. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Page 66 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Spectacular and Exceedingly Rare Gold Gulden
11157
DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Rhinsk Gylden), 1527. Malmö Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU Details—Edge Filing. Fr-10; 
Galster-59; Sieg-35; Jensen & Skjoldager-T-22/26; Schou-2; Bruun-4057. Weight: 3.18 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. 
Lustrous, superbly struck and with only a slight trace of filing at the outer rim to fault this otherwise spectacular early 
Danish gold piece. Excessively rare with only about a handful known in public as well as private collections.
Two variants, by two different engravers and struck from three different sets of dies, are known from the production of this 
issue. The present is stylistically superior, especially in the rendering of the standing king.
Whereas the obverse closely copies a design from his predecessor, the four shields on the reverse surrounding the national 
coat of arms with the three lions symbolizes different parts of the realm: Schleswig (2 lions), Holstein (a nettle leaf), the 
Wends (a wyvern - a mythological dragon) and Norway (an axe-bearing, upright lion).
€40,000-€50,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 194 in his collection 
and illustrated on p. 187 in the catalog.

11158

rare and the only type featuring a real portrait of Frederik I that played a genuine role in the monetary circulation.
€10,000-€15,000

11159
DENMARK. Gotland. Skilling, 1525. Visby Mint. Christian II. 
NGC MS-61. Galster-143; Sieg-12; Llt-3; Delzanno-79; Schou-2; 
Bruun-4114. Weight: 4.24 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Seyer. Excep-
tionally well-preserved with most of the original silvering intact. 
Rare Siege type struck for the admiral and governor of Gotland, 
Søren Norby during the war between Christian II and his uncle, 
Frederik I. 
Norby’s use of the Danish Coat of Arms with the three lions leaves 
no doubt that he saw the island of Gotland as an integrated part 
of Denmark.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

0.41 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. Excessively rare and histori-
cally interesting one-year type struck for Christian II during the 
so-called Count’s Feud, a war of succession that followed the 
death of Frederik I.

11161
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, ND (1535). Malmø or Copenhagen 
Mint. Christian II. NGC MS-62. Galster-91; Sieg-5; Schou-3; 
Bruun-4149. Weight: 3.56 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock. Struck 
in the name of the German Count, Christopher of Oldenburg 
(1504 - 1566) who gave name to the Count’s feud. Christopher 
sided with the former king, Christian II in the war against Chris-
tian (III) and acted as regent in the eastern Denmark between 
1534 and 1536. A very difficult type to obtain in any condition. 
€700-€1,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
11162
DENMARK. 8 Skilling (klippe), 1535. Aarhus Mint. Christian 
III. NGC AU-55. Galster-93; Sieg-12; Schou-239; Bruun-4156. 
Weight: 2.72 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. Sublimely struck 
example, very rare thus.
This unusual klippe was issued as payment for the mercenary 
troops hired by Christian (III) during the war of succession be-
tween Christian (III) and his cousin Christian II. It was struck in 
Aarhus, where the former mintmaster of Gottorp, Reynold Junge 
had established a new mint, as the elected king needed one within 
the kingdom itself. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. H. Ernst (Copenhagen - 1915) Lot # 35 (illustrated plate 4)
    
11163
DENMARK. Mark, 1535. Roskilde Mint. Christian III. 
NGC Unc Details—Obverse Scratched. Galster-98; Sieg-13; 
Schou-28; Bruun-4194. Weight: 8.07 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold 
Junge. Although somewhat double-struck, the present example 
is vastly superior to what is normally seen at auctions. An unusu-
ally nice portrait, struck on a sound flan with considerable luster 
remaining.
During the Count’s Feud, the Mint had to move with the army, and 
in August 1535 Reynold Junge established a new Mint at the royal 
court in Roskilde. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Wonderfully Pedigreed Joachimsdaler
11165
DENMARK. Joachimsdaler (Taler), 1537. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Dav-
8226A; Galster-102; Sieg-19; Schou-13; Bruun-4278. Weight: 28.80 gms. Mintmaster: Reynold Junge. This wonderfully 
pedigreed example is a rare variant with the date above the shield.
This early Danish Taler struck the year after the Danish Reformation was modeled upon the silver coins minted in the Ger-
man city of Joachimsthal by the counts of Schlick.

Page 70 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
    
     
11166
DENMARK. Mark, 1541. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III. NGC EF Details—Obverse Scratched. Hede-3A; Sieg-
37.1; Schou-2; Bruun-4282. Weight: 9.92 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. In spite of the flan flaw and scratch on the 
obverse this is a most pleasant example with a nice toning. This first and very scarce date was the only one minted with 
a high silver content of c. 90%.
This type is normally considered to mark (no pun intended) the introduction of the “modern” period in Danish numismatics. 
It was minted in the center of Copenhagen at the convent of St. Clare which had been abandoned after the Reformation in 
1536. Today, the two streets meeting where the convent once stood are named ‘Gammel Mønt’ and ‘Møntergade’ (‘Old Mint’ 
and ‘Mint Street’). 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11167
DENMARK. 4 Skilling, 1546. Copenhagen Mint. Christian III. NGC AU Details—Bent. Hede-5B; Sieg-35.2; Schou-5; 
Bruun-4309. Weight: 2.19 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. A hairline crack in the flan aside the coin presents itself nicely 
with superior details on both sides. Excessively rare and in all likelihood the only example in private hands. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: August Christian Mohr Collection (Copenhagen - 1847) Lot # 2235.

DENMARK. Mark, 1559. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-53. Hede-9A; Sieg-15.1; Schou-1; Bruun-4398. 
Weight: 9.59 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. A sublime example presenting a lovely toning with appealing hues of blue. 
Rare date and the only one struck in accordance to the 1544 decree of Christian III. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 89 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 71
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Unique in Private Hands and Exceptionally Pedigreed
11169
DENMARK. 3 Mark, 1560. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC VF Details—Graffiti. Dav-8228; Hede-8A; Sieg-21; 
Schou-1; Bruun-4402. Weight: 28.10 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Fechtel. Engraved initials in lower reverse field. With an 
original minting of only 16 examples this is almost a miraculous find, and it is today believed to be unique in private 
hands with the only other known example resting at the National Museum. 
The extremely low mintage was due to the fact that the king opposed the minting of Speciedalers as it was simply not profit-
able. The rather common 1563 Speciedaler (bearing the date in Roman numerals) were minted from private funds.
Count Brahe acquired this outstanding coin through a trade-off with the Royal Coin Cabinet.
€8,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex. Danish Royal Collection of Coins and Medals.
Ex: Henrik Hielmstierne Collection (Private Sale - 1812); published in 1786 as No. 3 (page 237) in his collection.

========== PAGE 90 ==========

Page 72 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
    
11170
DENMARK. Mark Klippe, 1563. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik 
II. NGC EF Details—Reverse Graffiti. Hede-14B; Sieg-16.2; 
Schou-10; Bruun-4451. Weight: 9.47 gms. Mintmaster: Poul 
Fechtel. Extremely rare type with the date on the obverse in a 
condition decidedly better than the only other example that has 
been on the market (from the Hede, Lerche and Bonde collec -
tions).
These primitive and base coins, which were minted during the 
Northern Seven Years’ War, were so badly regarded by the popula-
tion that the king had to erect gallows around the country in order 
to threaten the death penalty if anyone refused to accept the king’s 
coin at face value. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11171
DENMARK. , 1572. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-
50. Dav-8229; Hede-17; Sieg-22; Schou-10; Aagaard & Märch -
er-72.1; Bruun-4525. Weight: 29.07 gms. Mintmaster: Hans 
Delhusen. Engraver: Cort Dellhusen. Very nice example struck 
on an unusually fine flan and boasting a charming, iridescent 
toning.
This beautiful type, which for the first time carries the three crowns 
of Sweden in the Danish royal arms, was struck from silver paid by 
the Swedish king to reacquire the fortress, Elfsborg, which had been 
conquered by the Danes during the Northern Seven Years War.  
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Dr. Antoine-Feill Collection (Joseph Hamburger, Frankfurt am 
Main - 1907) Lot # 11.

DENMARK. Hvid, 1579. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik II. NGC AU-55. Hede-22; Sieg-1; Schou-16; Bruun-4533. 
Mintmaster: Hans Dellhusen. A delightful example with the most enchanting luster. Extremely rare one-year type from

11173
DENMARK. Mark, 1582. Frederiksborg Mint. Frederik II. NGC VF Details—Tooled. Hede-23; Sieg-18; 
Schou-2; Bruun-4534. Weight: 8.62 gms. Mintmaster: Poul Gulden. Engraver: Christopher Angerer. The pres-
ent example of this highly interesting type, which is presumed unique in private hands, was in the Royal Danish 
Collection in the mid 19th cent. before it was traded with Count Bille-Brahe. 
This type is from the first series struck at the newly opened Mint in the Frederiksborg Deer Garden northwest of 
Copenhagen. This was the first time roller dies were used in Denmark. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 92 ==========

Page 74 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Highly Important and Unique in Private Ownership
11174
DENMARK. Half Portugaloser (5 Ducats), 1593. Haderslev Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Mount Re -
moved. Fr-63; Hede-6; Sieg-160; Harck-2.7; Schou-1; Bruun-4600. Weight: 17.32 gms. Mintmaster: Andreas Metzner. 
Exceptionally well-struck on an even flan. With an outstanding issue as the present it is by no means surprising that 
there is evidence of a contemporary mounting positioned right above the royal coat of arms presumably to be used as 
a medallion by high ranking dignitaries. In fact, most of the surviving Haderslev gold issues have traces of mounting. 
According to the most recent research (Harck) this is the only known example in private hands with only a couple being 
publicly held.
This numismatically and historically highly important and weighty gold type was struck on the orders of the dowager queen 
Sophia on behalf of her son, who was only 16 at the time and had not yet been crowned. The design is a relatively close copy 
of the original Portuguese gold coins that gave their name to this popular trade coin. Following a complaint by the Council 
of State the central heraldic field differs from the otherwise basically similar first series (1591-1592) in having the two bars 
of Oldenburg and the cross of Delmenhorst in the center of the coat of arms instead of the horseman of Ditmarschen (Dit-
marsken).
€60,000-€80,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Christiane Lugge Collection (Copenhagen - 1818) Lot # 1.
JOHAN ANDREAS FERDINAND WOLFF (1814-1893)
Johan Andreas Ferdinand Wolff (1814-1893) was a bank director (Bikuben), 
merchant, and Danish consul in Manila. His collection was cataloged by Peter 
Hauberg (later to become the managing director of the Royal Coin Cabinet) 
and sold in 1882.

========== PAGE 93 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 75
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Interesting and Desirable Largesse Money
11175
DENMARK. Mark, 1596. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Hede-69; Sieg-63; Harck-4.2; Schou-5; Bru-
un-4625. Weight: 9.57 gms. Mintmaster: Gert van Campen. Very few examples are known in private hands of which 
this is far and away the best.
This historically important one-year type was struck as largesse money for the coronation on 29 August 1596.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11176
DENMARK. Penny, 1602. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62 Brown. Hede-89; Sieg-1; Harck-6.39; 
Schou-22; Bruun-4645. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. This highly unusual, uniface copper type was the last date this 
very small denomination (1/12 Skilling) was minted. Not surprisingly, it is very hard to come by in a decent condition, 
let alone a first-rate piece as the present. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Johan Andreas Ferdinand Wolff (Copenhagen - 1882) Lot # 418.

========== PAGE 94 ==========

Page 76 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
    
11177
DENMARK. 8 Solidi, 1603. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-15; Hede-74; Sieg-52; Harck-6.23.2; 
Schou-8; Bruun-4650. Weight: 3.20 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Engraver: Nicolaus Schwabe. A truly magnifi-
cent example with normally lost details and a blazing luster beneath an alluring iridescent toning. Scarce variant without 
curlicue below the elephant.
This beautiful and amusing type was according to tradition struck as largesse money to be used in connection with the cel-
ebration of the Hanseatic city of Hamburg swearing allegiance to the Danish king on 30 October 1603. 
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Very Rare and Sharply Struck Gold Klippe
11178
DENMARK. 6 Daler Klippe, 1604. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-53. Fr-45; KM-26; Hede-11; Sieg-147; 
Schou-3; Bruun-4668. Weight: 13.05 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. A very nice example with all four corners 
sharply struck. Of the originally 143 examples minted few still exist.
Two different obverse dies were used with markedly different portraits of which this, resembling the one used for minor 
coins like the Soesling, Hede-82, is by far the rarest. At the time the value of 6 Daler corresponded to 3.5 Ducats (Hungarian 
Guldens).
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

DENMARK. Gold Gulden (Ungersk Gylden), 1611. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC VF Details—Cleaned. Fr-

Spectacular and Charming Elephant Type
11186
DENMARK. Rose Noble, 1612. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: clover. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-48; KM-51; 
Hede-23A; Sieg-143.1; Schou-2; Bruun-4865. Weight: 8.93 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Delicate purplish hues 
further enhances the incredible eye-appeal of this magnificent piece boasting a splendid luster and wonderful details. 
The inspiration for this beautiful, amusing and very rare type came from the British Rose Nobles that were widely used 
in international trade. In the contemporary official accounts the type is referred to as a “Guldridder” from the Dutch, 
“Gouden rijder” .
€90,000-€130,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 174.
