========== PAGE 98 ==========

Page 80 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Superlative Provincial Shield Type
11192
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, 1624. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-58. Dav-3525; KM-105; Hede-61A; 
Sieg-105.1; Harck-23.5.3; Schou-8; Bruun-5368. Weight: 58.28 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. Imposing Double 
Taler with a remarkable luster and superlative details. The beautiful type with the curved, so-called “German” provincial 
shields is very rare.
In an entry of March 31st 1624 the king wrote in one of his diaries that he had received 140 Speciedalers from Schwabe 
which he had struck from Norwegian silver. Even though there might have been silver from other sources in the alloy there 
can be little doubt that the beautiful 1624-series is the first of the Danish / Norwegian coins to be struck from silver actually 
mined at Kongsberg Silver Works which was founded shortly after finding silver ores in the cliffs in the summer of 1623.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 99 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 81
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Presumably Only Example in Existence
11193
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1624. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-5371; KM-103; 
Hede-60B; Sieg-104.2; Harck-23.2; Schou-17; Bruun-5371. Weight:28.79 gms. Mintmaster: Nicolaus Schwabe. A few 
minor obverse surface marks aside, this is an absolutely gorgeous example with a choice striking and a remarkable lus-
ter. The present specimen of this beautiful Taler type is with all likelihood unique as no other examples neither publicly 
held or in private collections have been recorded. 
The issue should probably be considered a pattern that was realized as the crown on the reverse still had the old “open” 
design as opposed to the obverse where the king is wearing a “closed” crown.
€12,000-€16,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Schwabe. An insignificant scratch can be seen underneath the crown on the reverse. This one-year type “broad” Krone is 
notably difficult to acquire in a nice condition, as they are very often worn or weakly struck. The present example however 
is unusually attractive and scarce thus.

Excessively Rare Wolfenbüttel Type
11200
DENMARK. Lower Saxony. Gutergroschen, 1627. Wolfenbüttel Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55. KM-65.2; Hede-
6B; Sieg-192.2; Harck-27.8.1; Schou-15; Aagaard-15.3 (this example); Bruun-5538. Struck for count Philipp Reinhard 
von Solms-Hohensolms (1613-1635) as viceregent of the Danish King. In spite of the reverse being somewhat double-
struck this is a much better example than anyone could reasonably hope for. Extremely rare as a type and with no other 
examples recorded of the present variant (Schou-15).

11201
DENMARK. Gold 2 Krone, 1628. Copenhagen Mint; Privy mark: flower. Christian IV . NGC Unc Details—Obverse 
Scratched. Fr-54; KM-74.2; Hede-25D; Sieg-134.4; Schou-5; Harck-29.1; Bruun-5542. Weight: 5.98 gms. Mintmaster: 
Peter Grüner. A superficial scratch can be seen in the reverse field above the crown, but otherwise a wonderfully lus -
trous example and decidedly better than the only other one known in private hands (Count Lerche Collection, 1992, lot 
661). This date is the only one struck for Peter Grüner who went on to become the mintmaster of Christiania, Norway 
from 1643 until his death in 1650.

Schou-7; AH-32.8.2; Bruun-5629. Weight: 14.49 gms. Mintmas-

dalers are excessively rare. Dark, slightly patchy blue toning on

€5,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex. Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot# 617.
Ex. Peter Fr. Suhm Collection (Copenhagen - 1802) Lot# 1625.
PETER VON HEMMERT / V AN HEMERT 
(1734-1810) 
 
 
Peter von Hemmert / van Hemert (1734-1810), was a mer -
chant and shipowner who functioned as one of the directors 
in the Danish Asiatic Company from 1776 to 1783. Partly 
due to the general economic downturn for merchant ships 
following the end of the American Revolutionary War and 
partly due to an embezzlement scandal at the Asiatic Com-
pany, von Hemmert was financially brought to his knees. 
The downfall culminated in 1805 when his company was 
taken into bankruptcy. Just two years later, he was forced to 
sell his coin collection, which was astoundingly rich in large 
and excessively rare gold coins, at auction.


========== PAGE 105 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 87
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Highly Interesting Tetragrammaton Pattern
11205
DENMARK. 20 Skilling, 1644. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. KM-PnA8; Hede-146; 
Sieg-72.2; Schou-58; Aagaard-KP1; Bruun-5697. Weight: 8.18 gms. Clearly struck and nicely re-toning following a 
gentle, old cleaning. Excessively rare and highly interesting. 
This pattern was never used for circulation for at least two reasons: The Hebrew tetragrammaton legend rendered the V 
incorrectly, and although the value would be on a par with the old Rigsmark, the denomination of 20 in a duodecimal 
system would be impractical.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
11204
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1640. Glückstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. Dav-3670; KM-27; 
Hede-163A; Sieg-186.1; Harck-34.3.1; Schou-11; Lange-38; Bruun-5634. Weight: 28.94 gms. Mintmaster: Simon Timpfe. 
Although lightly cleaned a centennial ago, nicely re-toning and lustrous with a nicely modelled figure of Fortuna.
Extremely rare one-year type, where the engraver accidentaly has made the mistake that the wind blows Fortuna’s hair and 
the pennant in opposite directions. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 106 ==========

Page 88 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
    
11206
DENMARK. Ducat, 1645. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-58. Fr-39; KM-141; Hede-33; Sieg-128.2; 
Harck-35.3.2; Schou-2; Aagaard-KG5; Bruun-5731. Weight: 3.52 gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Engraver: Hans 
zum Busch. Although this type and date doesn’t belong among the rarities as such, the present example hovers way above 
the conditions normally encountered.
In the middle of the Torstenson War and possibly to invoke the help of God against the Swedes, king Christian personally 
sketched the coin design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and gold 
coins struck in Copenhagen, Christiania, and Glückstadt. 
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Extremely Rare Hebrew Glückstadt Half Ducat
11207
DENMARK. 1/2 Ducat, 1645. Glückstadt Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-62. Fr-unlisted; KM-35; Hede-155; Sieg-
188; Harck-38.3; Schou-10; Aagaard-GG3; Bruun-5737. Weight: 1.73 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger. While the 
“Hebrew” Ducats minted in Glückstadt during the reign of Christian are all rare, the Half Ducats are exceptionally so, 
and with less than a handful in private ownership the present eminently lustrous and highly detailed example can’t be 
bettered.
€8,000-€10,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

166; Sieg-180; Harck-38.6; Schou-28; Lange -; Bruun-5758. Weight: 6.94 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schwieger. Scratches in

Very Rare and Imposing Hebrew Type Double Ducat
11209
DENMARK. 2 Ducat, 1646. Copenhagen Mint; privy mark: Poker.. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Fr-38; 
KM-140; Hede-32; Sieg-131.2; Schou-1; Aagaard-KG2; Bruun-5781. Weight: 6.90 gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. 
Although minor edge nicks are present, this example presents very well on an unusually even flan.
In the middle of the Torstenson War and possibly to invoke the help of God against the Swedes, king Christian personally 
sketched the coin design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and 
gold coins struck in Copenhagen, Christiania, and Glückstadt. The 2 Ducat, which was the largest denomination of a total 
of 7 (four in gold and three in silver), is by far the rarest and is only seldom seen in the market.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenhagen - 1899) Lot # 913.

========== PAGE 108 ==========

Page 90 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
“Spectacular” and Excessively Rare Half Ducat
11210
DENMARK. 1/2 Ducat, 1647. Copenhagen Mint. Christian IV . NGC Unc Details—Bent. Fr-59; KM-151; Hede-38; 
Sieg-125; Harck-36.5; Schou-5; Bruun-5815. Weight: 1.74 gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum 
Busch. Excessively rare and highly attractive with a clean strike, a light luster and only a slight waviness of the flan 
as usual. Due to its “spectacular” design this type has always ranked among the most coveted of all from the Danish 
Renaissance. The latin inscription, VIDE MIRA DOMI (“Behold the wonders of the Lord”) found below a pair of spec-
tacles refers to the gold that was found near Arendal in Norway.
The king had high hopes for this find and had this highly unusual series of Ducats made from the Norwegian gold which 
was brought to Copenhagen to be refined by the alchemist and goldsmith, Caspar Herbach.
€18,000-€22,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 359.
Ex: L. Christian Nielsen Collection (Copenhagen - 1878) Lot # 29.

========== PAGE 109 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 91
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
11211
DENMARK. 2 Gold Krone, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Chris -
tian IV . NGC Unc Details—Damaged, Cleaned. Fr-54; KM-
74.2; Hede-25E; Sieg-134.5; Schou-3; Bruun-5829. Weight: 5.91 
gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Five very small drill-holes 
are not easily explicable but might just have been done to test the 
gold. Otherwise a most attractive example perfectly struck on an 
even flan. Christian IV died early in 1648 (February 28th), and 
the present very rare Double Gold Krone is the only one minted 
by Heinrich Köhler. 
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
11212
DENMARK. Denning, ND (1619-1620). Glückstadt Mint. 
Christian IV . NGC AU-50. KM-6.2; Hede-169; Sieg-166.2; 
Harck-17.3.1; Schou-80; Bruun-5937. Mintmaster: Albert Dio-
nis. Nicely toned example of one of the rare and interesting wire 
money coinage minted to be used for trade in the Pechora River 
region of Russia. 
€600-€1,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
11213
DENMARK. 1/2 Ducat, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik 
III. NGC AU-53. Fr-76; KM-162; Hede-6; Sieg-104; Schou-29; 
Aagaard-7.1; Bruun-5984. Weight: 1.68 gms. Mintmaster: Hen-
rik Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. Flan slightly wavy as al-
ways. Scarce.
For the celebration of the king’s coronation on the 23rd of Novem-
ber a series of unusual coins were minted on square flans to be used 
as largesse money. Silver coins with a total value of 1000 Rigsdalers 
were minted in denominations of 1/4, 1/6 and 1/12 Speciedaler to 
be thrown to the crowd in the streets of Copenhagen. It has been 
speculated that the reason for making the coin square was so they 
would not roll down the open sewers. Gold coins were also minted 
as Ducats and 1/2 Ducats that might have been used in the same 
way, whereas the 5, 4, 3 and 2 Ducats were minted for the personal 
use of the king and the chancellor, Corfitz Ulfeldt.The reverse has 
been interpreted as either an urn spewing ashes or as an incense 
burner. On the receptacle is written, TANDEM, the Latin word for 
“finally” which might refer to the fact that Christian IV , after nearly 
60 years on the throne, had finally gone to meet his maker. 
€1,800-€2,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11214
DENMARK. 1/12 Speciedaler, 1648. Copenhagen Mint. 
Frederik III. NGC MS-63. KM-159; Hede-49; Sieg-64; 
Schou-36; Aagaard-69.3 (48-2v/48.3); Bruun-5991. Weight: 2.43 
gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. A 
sublime, iridescent toning further enhances the spectacular lus-
ter and makes this a most unusual condition for a largesse klippe. 
€700-€900
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 110 ==========

Page 92 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Exceedingly Rare and Absolutely Stunning Quintuple Ducat
11215
DENMARK. 5 Ducats, 1653. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-61. Fr-98; KM-Pn10; Hede-12; Sieg-113; 
Schou-5; Aagaard-13.2 (53-1/53.2); Bruun-6084. Weight: 17.30 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engraver: Jeremias 
Hercules. Less than a handful of examples are known of this hefty gold type of which the present example is surely the 
best with its ravishing luster and awe-inspiring details.
These weighty gold types were probably made on the request of wealthy merchants for large international payments, and 
in the present case (indicated by the von Hemmert provenance) made for the king’s personal use. The same obverse die was 
used to strike both 1, 2, 5 and 10 Ducats and while large multiple-Ducats were normally struck with a larger diameter or 
even with Taler dies, this emission is an extremely rare example of an exceedingly thick gold coin.
€40,000-€60,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 69.
    
11216
DENMARK. 4 Mark (Krone), 1653. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3570A; KM-
192.2; Hede-91B; Sieg-46.2; Schou-39; Aagaard-17.1; Bruun-6100. Weight: 21.91 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. A 
very pleasing example with an unusually even strike.
It is presumed the adding of the chain of the French Order of St. Michael was due to the king being initiated into the order, 
however no further evidence have been found to collaborate the theory. 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Extremely Rare and Hefty Double Taler  
with an Illustrious Provenance
11217
DENMARK. 2 Speciedaler, 1655. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3543; KM-205; 
Hede-55; Sieg-74; Schou-7; Aagaard-82.1; Bruun-6178. Weight: 57.03 gms. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Engravers: Jer-
emias Hercules (obverse), Matthias Först (reverse). Although at some point gently wiped, a genuinely extraordinary 
example with a lovely re-toning, incredible details and an impressive luster. Extremely rare.
This example was formerly in the collection of the esteemed Chancellor of Justice, Jacob Gude (1754-1810) who is still well 
known today from his eyewitness report on the English bombardment of Copenhagen in 1807.
€7,000-€9,000

Attractive and Majestic Full-Rigged Ship Type
11219
DENMARK. 2 Ducat, 1657. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58+. Fr-112; KM-216.1; Hede-28A; Sieg-
133.1; Schou-3; Aagaard-20.1 (57-1/57-1); Bruun-6219. Weight: 6.93 gms. Mintmaster: Heinrich Köhler. Engraver: 
Matthias Först. A very rare date of this majestic type boasting remarkable eye-appeal.The beautifully rendered full-rigged 
ship on the reverse is used to symbolize the fact that the coin itself had been struck with gold brought back from overseas.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

11220
DENMARK. 2 Krone, 1657. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3571; KM-211; Hede-
93; Sieg-48; Schou-26; Aagaard-64.1; Bruun-6224. Weight: 44.52 gms. A highly detailed strike with plenty of original 
luster remaining. Mintmaster: Henrik Köhler. Excessively rare type struck with dies made for a 1 Krone (IIII MARCK) 
but of double weight. 
€4,000-€6,000

Students participating in the defence of Copenhagen 
on the night between 10 and 11 February 1659.
Oil painting by Vilhelm Rosenstand.

========== PAGE 114 ==========

Iconic and Excessively Rare Commemorative Type
11221
DENMARK. 4 Mark (Krone), 1659. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Plugged. Dav-3578; KM-
Pn15; Hede-102; Sieg-55; Schou-37; Bruun-6288. Weight: 21.43 gms. Excessively rare pattern only known in a handful 
of examples. Although the present coin has been plugged, the details are all excellent and traces of the original luster 
remains. The only other example known in private ownership was also previously in the Bruun collection but was sold 
as a duplicate in the 1925 (catalog # 2501).
This iconic type was struck to commemorate the failure of the Swedish king, Carl X Gustav, to capture Copenhagen. The 
monogram of King Frederik III is shown above a stone with the text EBEN EZER, referring to the Old Testament (1 Samuel 
7:12) in which Samuel erected a “stone of help” (Eben-ha-Ezer) between Mizpah and Jeshanah to honor the Lord for his help 
in securing the Israelites a victory over the Philistines.
The Latin inscription on the reverse, SOLI DEO GLORIA, is another tribute to God (“To God alone be the glory”) while the 
dramatic design shows the hand of God (Manus Dei) severing the hand of the Swedish king with the sword of justice as he 
reaches out for the Danish crown.
Normally considered a pattern, the varying weights of the few examples known combined with the fact that this example 
has clearly been carried, might suggest that it was struck to be used as a distinction given to personnel from the civic guard 
who had excelled themselves during the siege.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 1376 in his collection 
and illustrated on page 232 of the catalog.
Page 96 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III


========== PAGE 115 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 97
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Johan Peter Samuel Goldschmidt, who as a coin collector is best known by 
the surname Guildal, which he acquired in 1906, was born in Copenhagen in 
1855, the son of a shipbroker. After completing an apprenticeship in a whole-
saler’s company, he traveled to both England and Germany to study dyeing 
technology, so that on his return he was qualified to enter his uncle’s dye -
ing company, Goldschmidt and Nordholm. Together with the Swede Anders 
Nordholm, Johan Guildal took over the management as early as 1880; when 
the company changed to a limited company in 1906, he continued as director 
until 1919. At the end of the 19th 
century and the beginning of the 
20th, Guildal was a central figure 
within Danish industry and crafts-
manship and held board positions 
in many important companies 
such as Handelsbanken, The Dan-
ish Coal Company, Danish Lloyd, 
The Danish-Russian Steamship 
Company, Atlas Machine Factory 
and Portland Cement Factory.
In 1899, the same year he was awarded the Order of the Dannebrog, Guil-
dal participated in the nine-month long voyage to East Asia on board the 
protected cruiser, Valkyrien (“The Valkyrie”), under the command of the 
king’s youngest son, Prince Valdemar.  The trip had been advocated ex-
pressly by the Danish parliament at the urging of Danish business inter -
ests. On the way visits were made to both the Siamese king Chulalongkorn 
and the Japanese emperor Meiji.
Guildal was also a prominent art collector, and his collection of old master prints was sold by Winkel & Magnus -
sen in 1931. Prints from his collection (easily recognizable from the small ink stamp verso, Lugt 1110a) can now be 
found in museums all over the world, including at the Metropolitan Museum in New Y ork and the Rijksmuseum in 
Amsterdam.
His main passion, however, was his coin collection. He was one of the founding fathers of the first Danish society for 
coin collectors formed in 1885, where L. E. Bruun was also a member. From 1914 through 1916 he acted as chair -
man of the still existing Numismatisk Forening (Danish Numismatic Society). Unlike L. E. Bruun, he didn’t collect 
medieval coins, but instead focused on the issues struck by the House of Oldenburg, which ruled Denmark from 
1448 to 1863.
Given his interest in the graphic arts it was only natural that he also collected paper money. Whereas this collection 
of 562 pieces is today a part of the collection at the National Museum of Denmark, Guildal decided to sell his coin 
collection in its entirety to L. E. Bruun in June 1918. Guildal was not a stranger to the idea of trading large collections 
en bloc, as he had himself acquired all Major General Carl Thorvald Jørgensen’s (1819-1902) formidable collection 
of Danish and Norwegian coins in 1901.
Shortly before his death on December 21, 1920, Guildal’s collection was published as vol. III of “Numismatisk 
Forenings Medlemsblad” and cataloged by Hans Henrik Schou. 
J. G. GUILDAL
(1850-1920) 
J.G. Guildal, Danish Royal Library.
Royal Danish Order of the 
Dannebrog, Grand Cross Star.

========== PAGE 116 ==========

Page 98 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Extremely Rare Gold Krone
The Best in Private Hands
11222
DENMARK. Gold Krone, 1660. Glückstadt Mint; privy mark: crossed pokers. Frederik III. NGC MS-61. Fr-120; 
KM-40.2; Hede-145B; Sieg-160 (B); Schou-2; Aagaard-4.1 (57.1-60.4); Bruun-6344. Weight: 5.91 gms. Mintmaster: Ja-
cob Schwieger. Engraver: Matthias Först (obverse), Johan Blum (reverse). This excessively rare variant with the reverse 
legend reading counterclockwise is known in less than a handful of examples with this stunning beauty decidedly the 
best in private collections.
€10,000-€14,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

11224
DENMARK. Ducat, 1661. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. 
NGC MS-61. Fr-101; KM-236; Hede-16B; Sieg-116.2; Schou-9; 
Aagaard-31.2; Bruun-6385. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Jo-
han Stichmann. Engraver: Henrik Köhler (obverse), Johan Blum 
(reverse). This very rare one-year variant offers a great deal of 
enchanting eye appeal.
Re-using an old Köhler-die for the obverse, Stichmann had these 
Ducats struck in a large town-house in Købmagergade in the cen-
ter of Copenhagen. The house later became the home of the famous 
Danish-Norwegian author, Ludvig Holberg. 
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).


========== PAGE 118 ==========

Page 100 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Imposing Near Choice Quadruple Ducat
One of Only Two Examples Known
11225
DENMARK. 4 Ducats, 1663-IS. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC MS-62. Fr-116; KM-unlisted; Hede-61; Sieg-
79 (here wrongly listed as 1662); Schou-5; Aagaard-97.2 (62.1/63.1); Bruun-6399. Weight: 13.86 gms. Mintmaster: Jo-
han Stichmann. Engraver: Johan Blum. A flawless, shimmery Mint State example of this beautiful and literally majestic 
type. The only other example recorded, now in the National Museum of Denmark, was sold in the 1925 auction of 
Bruun duplicates as lot 2563.
The engraver Johan Blum (ca. 1599 - 1669) had been working as an engraver at the Mint in Bremen. In 1657 he was ap-
pointed medalist by Frederik III and married a Dane. He was technically very accomplished and artistically his work is 
inspired by Sebastian Dadler.
Gold pieces struck from the same pair of dies but with the weight of 10 and 5 Ducats are also known. Like the present they 
have very exact weights, clearly indicating that they were indeed issued as coins and presumably intended for the personal 
use of the king. 
€80,000-€100,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenhagen - 1899) Lot # 1296.

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 101

Sieg-106.2; Schou-11; Aagaard-42.1; Bruun-6418. Weight: 1.72 gms. Mintmaster: Caspar Herbach. Engravers: Matthias 
Först (obverse), Johan Blum (reverse). This variant which reuses an obverse die from the 1659 emission (Hede-15A) 
might very well be the only privately owned example. 
€2,500-€3,500

emias Hercules. A scarce date of this unusual type which quite unconventionally for a gold coin bears the denomination 
of 3 Mark (1/2 Taler) written all across the reverse.
It has been suggested that these very small gold coins were minted for the king to be used as handouts on his domestic travel.

11236
DENMARK. 1/2 Speciedaler, ND (1669). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-55. KM-Pn19; Hede-36; Sieg-
141; Schou-19; Aagaard-63.1; Bruun-6746. Weight: 14.50 gms. Mintmaster: Gotfred Krüger. Fine old cabinet toning. 
Extremely rare and enigmatic issue. It was struck with dies made for the 10 Ducat 1669 and might (together with the 
very few other examples of different weights, Schou-19a) be considered an off-strike in silver. The weight of the present 
singular example (Schou-19) is however consistent with what would be expected from a Half Taler and is by KM listed 
as a pattern.

This beautiful type was presumably struck at the behest of Norwegian Governor-general Ulrik Frederik Gyldenløve. He was 
the illegitimate son of Frederik III and resided permanently in Norway from June 1673 to November 1679.

variant where the horse is walking on grass. 
€800-€1,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11242
DENMARK. 1/4 Ducat (3 Mark), 1676. Copenhagen Mint. 
Christian V . NGC Unc Details—Bent. Fr-173; KM-360; Hede-
9; Sieg-65; Schou-2; Bruun-6838. Weight: 0.87 gms. Mintmaster: 
Gotfred Krüger. A very charming example of this very rare and 
interesting issue.
The obverse die was also used by Frederik Grüner in Christiania 
for a small run of hefty Ducats and Double Ducats (Hede-7, Hede-
8). 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Page 108 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Norwegian Triple Ducat of Mythological Rarity
11243
NORW AY . 3 Ducats, 1678. Christiania mint. Christian V . NGC VF-35. Fr-12C; KM-139; Hede-17; Sieg-75; NMD-
8A; Schou-2; Bruun-6884. Weight: 9.82 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner the Y ounger. A very pleasing example of this 
formidable coin of which only two have been recorded, the present and one in St. Petersburg. At the time of L. E. Bruun 
this almost mythological rarity was believed to have been struck in Copenhagen but both the style of the portrait and 
the elegant triple-monogram, so typical for Grüner in Christiania, make it clear that it is a Norwegian issue.
€20,000-€40,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex. Schmidt Collection (1830) Lot # 98. 

========== PAGE 127 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 109
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Scarce and Remarkable Portrait
11244
DENMARK. Ducat, 1680. Glückstadt Mint. Christian V . NGC Unc Details—Bent. Fr-141; KM-70.1; Hede-117; cf. 
Sieg-144.3-4; Schou-1; Aagaard-6.1; Bruun-6910. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Christopher Woltereck. A remarkable 
example of an excessively rare variant with a scarce portrait and the mintmaster’s initials upside down at the top crown 
instead of next to his mintmark with the two crossed pokers.
Across the series of Ducats struck at Glückstadt between 1672 and 1685, the portraits of Christian V are numerous, with 
varying degrees of true likeness. It therefore seems likely that a number of different (unnamed) engravers and goldsmiths 
were employed when required by a given demand for gold.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
    
11245
DENMARK. 1/16 Speciedaler (3 Skilling Lybsk), 1680. Glückstadt Mint. Christian V . NGC EF Details—Bent. KM-
69; Hede-124; Sieg-132; Schou-32; Lange-83; Bruun-6919. Mintmaster: Christoper Woltereck. A nicely toned example 
of this singularly rare one-year type that Chr. Lange considered a Trial. It is the only minor coin with a portrait struck in 
Glückstadt during the reign of Christian V . 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 1821 in his collection.

Spectacular Example of a Very Rare Date
11246
DENMARK. Ducat, 1685-CW . Glückstadt. Christian V . NGC MS-61. Fr-41; KM-; Hede-117; Sieg-144.5; Schou-2; 
Aagaard-8.1; Bruun-7006. Weight: 4.44 gms. Mintmaster: Christopher Woltereck. Engraver: Unknown. A spectacular 
example of this very rare date of which no more than a handful is known (Aagaard mentions 6 in private hands, but E. 
Glückstadt # 2957, Schou # 741 and Seck # 54 are actually the same example being resold)
Across the series of Ducats struck at Glückstadt between 1672 and 1685, the portraits of Christian V are numerous, 
with varying degrees of true likeness. It therefore seems likely that a number of different (unnamed) engravers and 
goldsmiths were employed when required by a given demand for gold.
€7,000-€9,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 142.

Quaint and Excessively Rare Portrait Ducat
11247
DENMARK. Ducat, 1691. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC AU-58. Fr-156; KM-415.1; Hede-31A; Sieg-104.1; 
Schou-7; Bruun-7061. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Elder. Light golden toning and consider-
able remains of luster. Excessively rare issue with only about a handful still in existence.
The die for the portrait of this amusing Ducat which was struck between the death of Jeremias Hercules and the appoint-
ment of Anton Meybusch was clearly cut by an engraver without the best artistic qualifications.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 1947 in his collec-
tion.

ing. This hefty coin which was presumably struck as a presentation piece of the newly designed Krone (Hede-90A) have 
only been recorded in three examples, the present, one at the National Museum of Denmark, and one in St. Petersburg.

Impressive Double Ducat
One of Only Two Known
11251
DENMARK. 2 Ducat, 1694. Copenhagen Mint. Christian V . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Fr-cf. 143; KM-Unlisted; 
Hede-36; Sieg-109; Schou-2; Kold-146a; Bruun-7160. Weight: 6.86 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Elder. En-
graver: Anton Meybusch. Albeit at some point lightly cleaned a distinguished example of this hefty Double Ducat. One 
of the great rarities among the gold coins of Christian V with no other examples recorded in private hands and only one 
in public collections (National Museum of Denmark).
The elegant and intriguing reverse shows the Triangle of the Trinity containing 3 Hebrew letters YOD at the center of three 
double C’s.

has remained anonymous.

issue minted during the Swedish siege of Christianstad (Kris -
tianstad) Fortress during the Scanian War. Although these prim-
itive issues are not really rare, stunningly preserved examples as 
the present are practically never seen on the market.

14.48 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Wilhelm von Meerheim. Uniface 
emergency klippe struck in lead during the Swedish siege of 
Christianstad (Kristianstad) Fortress during the Scanian War. 
As lead is extremely susceptible to both physical and corrosive 
damage finding a survivor in the present superlative condition 
is ultra rare.

Page 116 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Excessively Rare Danish West India Company Ducat
11263
DENMARK. Ducat, 1701. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC Unc Details—Bent. Fr-245; KM-unlisted; Hede-9; 
Sieg-41; Schou-4; Kold-155a; Bruun-7308. Weight: 3.47 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. Engraver: 
Anton Meybusch. Nick on edge. Excessively rare one-year type with only a very few in private ownership. 
The interesting reverse design which Meybusch had already used in 1699 during the reign of Christian V , shows a ship lying 
in front of the Christiansborg fort in Guinea. On the stern is seen the initials, DWC (Dansk Westindisk Compagnie / Danish 
West India Company) of the trading company that had commissioned this issue.
The Danish West India Company was formally chartered by King Christian V in 1671. The company administered the 
Danish West Indies until 1754, when it passed to the Danish Government. It played a vital part in the North Atlantic trian-
gular trade where enslaved Africans from the Gold Coast were traded for molasses and rum in the West Indies. The Danish 
West Indies, which were eventually sold to the United States in 1917 (presently U.S. Virgin Islands), consisted of St. Thomas 
(settled in 1668), St. John (settled in 1671/1718) and St. Croix (bought from the French in 1733).
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 135 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 117
Session I • Wednesday , October 29, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Wonderfully Pedigreed Speciedaler
Only Two Known
11264
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-1288; KM-480.2; 
Hede-34B; Sieg-24.2; Schou-7; Bruun-7333. Weight: 28.45 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. Superbly 
struck and with a smart toning along the rim after an old cleaning. In total only 309 examples were struck of the 1704 
Speciedaler financed by Christian Wineke the younger himself. Of the present variant with a plain edge and struck with 
an obverse die for the “Travel Taler” (Sieg 23.1-2) only two examples seem to be known today, the one in the National 
Museum and this particular piece with an amazing pedigree dating back to the end of the 18th century.
€18,000-€22,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: C. D. von Kreber Collection (Copenhagen - 1841) Lot # 2550.
Ex: C. F. Schubart (Copenhagen - 1831) Lot # 1851.
Ex: Peter v. Hemmert Collection (Copenhagen - 1807) Lot # 1495.

========== PAGE 136 ==========

Page 118 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
    
11265
DENMARK. 6 Mark (Rejsedaler), 1704. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-58. KM-cf. 479.2; Dav-1289; NMD-
1B; Hede-38; Sieg-23.3; Schou-9; Bruun-7335. Weight: 26.79 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. This is the 
scarcest of the three variations which is very difficult to find as attractive as the present with its excellent, cabinet toning 
and pleasant luster.
Struck for the king’s travel to Norway which lasted from the 29th of May until the 5th of September 1704.
The reverse bears an inscription by the famous Danish hymn writer, Thomas Kingo: MOD, TROSKAB, DAPPERHED OG 
HVAD DER GIVER AERE DEN HEELE VERDEN KAND BLANDT NORSKE KLIPPER LAERE (Courage, fidelity, bravery 
and what else brings honor can be learnt among the cliffs of Norway). The tradition of striking so-called, “Travel-Talers” in 
relation to the official royal travels to Norway was continued by the following three kings. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Appealing One-Y ear Trade Ducat

Schou-1; Lange-99Aa; Bruun-7348. Weight: 3.43 gms. Mintmaster: Christopher Woltereck. A very appealing example 
of this extremely rare one-year Trade Ducat that has the date on the reverse.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 2203 in his collec-
tion.

Wonderfully Lustrous and One of the Great Rarities of Frederik IV
11267
DENMARK. 2 Ducats, 1708. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik IV . NGC AU-55. Fr-211; KM-486; Hede-15; Sieg-44; 
Schou-2; Bruun-7353. Weight: 6.94 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. A wonderfully lustrous and pre-
cisely struck example of one of the great rarities among the coins of Frederik IV . This Double Ducat featuring an elegant 
reverse design with an alternating pattern of crowned arms and monograms was minted in both 1708 and 1709 but no 
other examples of any of the dates are known to be in private ownership today with only one example of each date in 
the Danish National Museum.

Stunning Ducat on the Royal Succession
11277
DENMARK. Ducat, ND (1746). Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-61. Fr-252; KM-547; Hede-1; Sieg-22; 
Schou-2; Bruun-7602. Weight: 3.45 gms. Mintmaster: Christian Wineke the Y ounger. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Ar-
bien (A). This scarce and stunning Ducat was struck to honor the succession of throne on August 6th 1746. As was 
customary the deceased king is wearing laurels, a tradition that continued on the Danish accession pieces through 1863.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Page 124 Stack’s Bowers Galleries

Near-Gem Fort Christiansborg Ducat
11278
DENMARK. Ducat, 1746. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik V . NGC MS-62. Fr-264; KM-552; Hede-7; Sieg-28; Schou-10; 
Bruun-7609. Weight: 3.46 gms. Mintmaster: Ingeborg Maria Wineke. Engraver: Magnus Gustavus Arbien (A). Superla-
tively attractive with brilliant fields and satiny semi-cameo devices.
The elegantly composed reverse very unusually bears a legend in Danish instead of Latin, as “FRA CHRISTIANSBORG” 
means “From Christiansborg”. It is evidently referring to the reverse imagery where a intricately detailed ship is seen leaving 
the harbor facing the fortress Christiansborg on the coast of Guinea.
€6,000-€8,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

11288
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1764. Copenhagen Mint. Frederik

cabinet toning further enhances the superior eye-appeal of this 
scarce one-year type.
The engraver Johan Heinrich Wahl, who has signed the excellent 
portrait with his initials, left Copenhagen in 1749 for a position at 
the Mint in Hamburg where he functioned until 1761. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11289
DENMARK. Krone, 1771. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. 
NGC MS-63. KM-622; Hede-23; Sieg-16; Schou-7; FP-E4; Bru-
un-7761. Weight: 17.89 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Schivern Knoph. 
Engraver: Johan Ephraim Bauert. Rare in this superlative condi-
tion.
This beautiful one-year type was struck in only 1,013 pieces for the 
king’s birthday on the 29th of January. Most were used as largesse 
money at the riding court at Christiansborg Castle.
The issue seems to have been instigated by the minister Johann 
Friedrich Struensee, who was also the mentally ill king’s physician. 
The following year Struensee was arrested, quartered and decapi-
tated for having an affair with the Queen, Caroline Mathilde. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11290

Extremely Attractive and Otherwise Unobtainable Pattern
11296
DENMARK. 1/3 Speciedaler, 1798. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC Unc Details—Cleaned. KM-653; Hede-
14; Sieg-21; FP-P1; Schou-3; Bruun-7938. Weight: 9.64 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Jacob Arnold Branth (HIAB). Engraver: 
Peter (Pietro) Leonard Gianelli (PG). Extremely attractive example with the superlative luster further enhancing the 
slightly mat devices. Highly interesting pattern struck in October 1798 as Gianelli’s first attempt as a coin engraver. It is 
only known in two examples (this and the one in the Danish National Museum) both of which have the same die flaw 
on the reverse and the same preparation of the flan (“cleaning”).
Gianelli was originally trained as a sculptor by renowned Danish artist Nicolai Abildgaard, and is credited for numerous 
medallic works throughout his career, the best known of which is the medal struck to commemorate the Danish abolition 
of the slave trade in 1792.
€8,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

11297
DENMARK. Speciedaler, 1800. Altona Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-62. Dav-1311; KM-138.5; Hede-39E; Sieg-45.5; 
Schou-2; FP-9.20; Lange-128; Bruun-7955. Weight: 28.85 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Peter (Pietro) Leon-
hard (Leonardo) Gianelli. Nicely toned and with practically no trace of circulation.
Trained as a sculptor by renowned Danish artist Nicolai Abildgaard, Pietro Gianelli is credited for numerous medallic and 
coinage works throughout his career, the best known of which is a medal struck to commemorate the Danish abolition of the 
slave trade in 1792. Speciedalers by his hand were issued at the Mints of Copenhagen, Altona and Kongsberg. 
€1,200-€1,600
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11298
DENMARK. Ducat, 1802. Copenhagen Mint. Christian VII. NGC MS-63. Fr-283; KM-650; Hede-2; Sieg-29; Schou-1; 
FP-47.4; Bruun-7974. Weight: 3.49 gms. Mintmaster: Michael Flor. Engraver: Georg Valentin Bauert. A remarkable ex-
ample with an excellent luster.
The so-called “Wildman Ducat” was the last Danish Ducat type produced with the 1802 being the very last date of issue. 
This type is the only Danish coin which gives the exact weight and fineness in the design, suiting it for international trade. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
11299
DENMARK. Rigsbankdaler, 1818. Altona Mint. Frederik VI. NGC MS-63. KM-683.4; Hede-24D; Sieg-26.4; Schou-1; 
Bruun-8044. Weight: 14.43 gms. Mintmaster: Cajus Branth (C.B.). Engraver: Johannes Conradsen (I.C.). A deliciously 
toned Gem example of one of the few types issued under Branth who only functioned from July 1817 until his early death 
in February 1819.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

One of the Rarest of All Danish 19th Century Coins
11300
DENMARK. 32 Skilling, 1818. Altona Mint. Frederik VI. NGC MS-62. KM-690.1; Hede-29A; Sieg-25.1; Schou-2; 
Bruun-8045. Weight: 6.13 gms. Mintmaster: Cajus Branth. Engraver: Georg Valentin Bauert. This one-year type is one 
of the rarest of all Danish 19th century coins with the present being the only recorded example outside the Danish Na-
tional Museum. Presenting itself in a subtly toned nearly-Choice condition this piece is bound to attract fierce bidding.
In the statute of July 31st 1818 it is mentioned that a coin with the value of 1/3 Riksbankdaler will be issued but as this didn’t 
happen until 1820, the present coin should be considered a pattern.
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Missing From Even the National Museum
11301

of only two known. A numismatically very important issue, which is clearly exemplified by the fact that when Bruun

A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History
Part III
           THE
L. E. BRUUN
              COLLECTION
Session II:  
Coins of Norw ay , Sweden & Germany

========== PAGE 156 ==========

Page 138 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
THE MEDIEV AL COINAGE OF NORW AY
COINS OF NORW AY
Like in the other Scandinavian countries, the first use of coins in Norway 
was based on the influx of foreign coins, beginning with early Imperial 
Roman Denarii, over the occasional Carolingian and Byzantine coins 
and the numerous Kufic Dirhems, to English and German Pennies from 
the turn of the millennium. A few, rare examples of Norwegian Pennies 
are known from the reigns of Olaf Tryggvason (968-1000) and Saint Olaf 
Haraldsson (1015-1028), but as opposed to Denmark, where local Mints 
from most major parts of the country were active through all of the 11th 
century, a proper national monetary system was not instituted in Norway 
until the middle of the century. 
Around 1050 Harald Hardrada introduced the triquetra Penny bearing 
a design inspired by contemporary Danish Pennies struck in the Royal 
city of Roskilde. Although the weight was more or less the same as the Danish Pennies, the silver content made them 
completely unusable in international trade, they soon replaced foreign coins and were valid currency everywhere 
in the kingdom. The overall finish of the dies, the lack of precision of the strikes etc. also clearly indicates that in 
contrast to Denmark, where Cnut had appointed skilled Anglo-Saxon moneyers to undertake the minting process, 
the actual minting in Norway was done by local silver smiths. During the reigns of his sons, Magnus and Olaf, the 
diversity of the imagery vastly expanded, and very often Anglo-Saxon, Danish and Byzantine elements were com -
bined to create motifs unique to Norway.
In the beginning of the reign of Magnus Barefoot towards the closing years of the 11th century a new coinage-reform 
saw the light of day, reducing the weight of the Penny by about a half, while at the same time considerably enhancing 
the silver content. Around 1110 the weight was however already halved once more, making the flans so thin that the 
design on both sides went through to the other side as a negative imprint creating a kind of semi-bracteates known 
from the Danish issues in 10th century Haithabu. The logical result soon became to issue only bracteates which be-
came completely dominant up until the reign of Magnus the Lawmaker (1263-1280). 
The series from around 1290 by Eirik Magnusson saw the introduction of the Axe-bearing Norwegian Lion in a 
shield, an image that would become dominant for centuries to come. 
In the dying years of the 13th century the duke Håkon Magnusson (V) tried to improve the currency with the intro-
duction of the “albi rosati” with a silver content of about 50% but the attempt was short-lived and towards the end 
of his reign as king, the Pennies (often referred to as “Black-Pennies”) which were minted in both Oslo and Bergen 
had a silver content of only 10 %.
From 1319 to 1343 Norway was ruled by the Swedish king, Magnus Eriksson, a grandson of Håkon V , and the two 
countries entered a monetary union. It can therefore sometimes be difficult with certainty to attribute some of the 
issues to either Norway or Sweden. The same case can be found regarding the bracteates from the late 12th up until 
the middle of the 13th century where final attribution of 
a number of types remains outstanding.
After the establishment of the personal union at Kalmar 
in 1397, uniting the Scandinavian countries under the 
Danish king, Norwegian coinage was discontinued for 
about a century before king Hans resumed local mint -
ing adhering to the Danish standard at Bergen, Nidaros 
(Trondheim) and Oslo.
Harald Hardrada window in Kirkwall Cathedral.
Sculpture of Håkon Magnusson
in Savanger Cathedral.
Eirik Magnusson, 1316-1374.

========== PAGE 157 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 139
Session II • Thursday , October 30, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Exceptionally Nice, Rare and Enigmatic
12001
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1047-66). Nidarnes (Trondheim) Mint. Harald III Hardrada (Hardråde). NGC Unc De-
tails—Peck Marked. Schive-cf. II:24; NM-3; NMH-14b; Skaare-62d; Bruun-8853; Weight: 0.88 gms. Hairline split in 
flan. Exceptionally well-struck, nicely toned and presenting an attractive luster. An extremely rare type with the enig-
matic obverse motif of a Triquetra (three interconnected arc shapes) that has roots in the ancient Celtic religion but was 
used on Danish coins by Cnut and Harthacnut.
Harald Hardrada (Severe / Resolute) is often thought of as the epitome of a Viking king, and his name is further immortal-
ized by several appearances in popular culture ranging from H. P . Lovecraft to the Netflix series “Vikings”.
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
12002
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1065-80). Olav Kyrre. NGC MS-62. Schive-III:17-20; NM-11; Stenersen-Class C, 20; Bru-
un-8860; Weight: 0.89 gms. A lovely toning with a hint of blue further enhances this piece’s superlative eye-appeal. 
€1,500-€2,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Gresli (Græslid) Hoard, Southern Trøndelag, 1878.

========== PAGE 158 ==========

Page 140 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Extremely Rare and Impressive Pointed Helmet Type
12003
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1080-95). Olav Kyrre. NGC MS-61. Schive-Unlisted; NM-8; NMH-49; Stenersen-Class L, 
cf. 3-5; Bruun-8873; Weight: 0.81 gms. Apart from two reverse spots of flaking, this is a magnificent example with an 
impressive portrait.
This extremely rare type with a bearded, facing head wearing a pointed helmet is believed to be struck towards the end of 
Olav Kyrre’s reign, and bears strong resemblance to the Hiberno-Norse imitations of Edward Confessor.
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Gresli (Græslid) Hoard, Southern Trøndelag, 1878.

Spectacular Anthropomorphic Animal Penny
12004
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1065-80). Olav Kyrre. NGC MS-64. Schive-III:31; NM-4; NMH-35; Stenersen-Class N, 15 
(Pl. IV , 94); Bruun-8876; Weight: 1.03 gms. An excessively rare type in a wonderful near-Gem condition with plenty of 
luster, a delicate luster and a perfect strike.
The enigmatic obverse showing an anthropomorphic animal (sea serpent ?) is highly unusual and uniquely Norse.

NORW AY . Penny, ND (1065-80). Olav Kyrre. NGC MS-63. Schive-II:34; NM-1; Stenersen-Class S, 75; Bruun-8894; Weight: 0.93 
gms. Moneyer: Gunnar. Superbly lustrous and excellently struck this piece is vastly superior to those usually found.

========== PAGE 162 ==========

Page 144 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Amazing Rarity with an Astounding Provenance
12010
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1093-1103). Unknown Mint. Magnus Berrføtt (Barefoot). NGC MS-64. Schive-T:7; NM-7; 
NMH-56; Josefsen-p. 98; Bruun-9021; Weight: 0.45 gms. A simply incredible survivor from one of the rarest and most 
sought-after of all Norwegian kings. With its amazing luster and lovely toning, this piece needs to be seen to be believed.
The provenance of this historically very interesting coin is second to non. It formed part of the only coin hoard ever found on 
the Faroe Island. The treasure that consisted of 98 silver coins buried between 1070 and 1080 was found in 1863 and pub-
lished shortly thereafter in "Annaler for nordisk Oldkyndighed og Historie" by the Danish Numismatist, Christian Frederik 
Herbst, as nr. 45 (Plate I, 2).
Magnus III Olafsson Barefoot is the last king to fall in battle abroad and is often referred to as the last Viking king of Nor-
way. He led several campaigns in the West and ensured Norwegian control over the Hebrides and Orkney. With all prob-
ability the treasure at Sandur was brought to the Faroes in connection with one of Magnus' campaigns.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Sandur (Sandø) Hoard, 1863.

========== PAGE 163 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 145
Session II • Thursday , October 30, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Three pages from the inventory of the Bille Brahe Collection 
handwritten in 1848 by Christian Frederik Herbst.

========== PAGE 164 ==========

Page 146 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
A Fabled Rarity with Considerable Eye Appeal
12011
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1177-1202). Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint. Sverre Sigurdsson. NGC MS-62. Schive-VI:73; 
NM-16 (1976); NM-1 (1992); NMH-115; Bruun-9058; Weight: 0.46 gms. An exquisite iridescent toning further en -
hances the considerable eye-appeal of this marvelous Medieval Penny struck with fully readable legends. The two-sided 
Pennies of Sverre Sigurdsson which closely imitates the Short Cross coinage of Henry II belong among the fabled rari-
ties of Norwegian numismatic history with very few in private ownership.
Most Norwegian 12th century coins are “mute” with the present type being a remarkable exception. The obverse reads: REX 
SVERUS MAGNUS, leaving no doubt as to the sovereign behind the issue. The reverse inscription is quite unusual and 
somewhat enigmatic: NI NI NI NI, but there can be little doubt that it refers to the city of Nidaros, present-day Trondheim, 
that had become an archbishopric in 1153.
Sverre was both an ordained priest and a highly skillful military leader, and is constantly ranked as one of the most impor-
tant Norwegian kings through history.
€12,000-€18,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: August Christian Mohr Collection (Copenhagen - 1847) Lot # 790.

========== PAGE 165 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 147
Session II • Thursday , October 30, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
12012
NORW AY . 1/4 Penny (?), ND (1177-1202). Bergen Mint (?). Sverre Sigurdsson. NGC AU-53. Schive-VI:90; NM-18:1 
(1976); NM-8 (1992); Bruun-9063; Weight: 0.06 gms. Hairline split in flan but nicely centered, toned and with excellent 
detail. 
Almost all of the Bracteates minted in the latter part of the 12th century were of the so-called “lettered” type, and the few ex-
ceptions, like the present with a crowned, facing head are extremely rare. This type seems to have struck over a longer period 
of time with varying weights, and the denomination is uncertain. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
One of Only a Few Types Attributed to Inge Haraldsson
12013
NORW AY . 1/2 Penny (Bracteate), ND (1136-61). Inge I Haraldsson (?). NGC MS-64. Schive-VII:55; NM-cf. 15/1 
(1976); Simensen-Fig. 73; Bruun-9098; Weight: 0.14 gms. A slight chip at the edge removes nothing from the fact that 
this is an absolutely stunning near Gem example with a lovely toning. Exceptionally rare, and one of the only Bracteates 
that carries a proper legend, R E X. 
This is one of the few types that has been attributed to Inge Haraldsson, known as Inge the Hunchback. A deformity he ac-
cording to the Danish chronicler, Saxo had gotten after being dropped on the floor by a maid during infancy. The style of the 
portrait is unusually good and bears resemblance to the Danish Bracteates from around 1150.
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 166 ==========

Page 148 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III

12014
NORW AY . 1/4 Penny, ND (1150-1205). Unknown Mint. Anonymous Bracteate. NGC MS-61. Schive-VIII:39; NM-27; 
NMH-107; Bruun-9160; Weight: 0.09 gms. A spectacular example of this very rare variant with the head of a crozier left. 
€1,000-€1,200
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Remarkably Preserved Magnus Lagabøter Penny
12015
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1263-80). Magnus Lagabøter. NGC EF Details - Environmental Damage. Schive-IX: cf. 1-2; 
NM-26 (1976); NM-2 (1992); NMH-216; Bruun-9164; Weight: 1.70 gms. This is the first two-sided type struck in Nor-
way after about one and a half century with only bracteates being issued. All coins struck for Magnus Lagabøter belong 
to the most highly coveted of Norwegian medieval coins, with examples as remarkably preserved as the present being 
considered unobtainable.
Magnus married the daughter of the Danish King, Erik Plovpenning, after practically having abducted her from a mon-
astery, but is otherwise known as a king who ruled by law rather than by the sword, hence his nickname, Lagabøter (the 
Lawmaker).
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Handsome and Very Rare “White Rose” Penny
12018
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1295-1299). Eirik II Magnusson. NGC EF-45. Schive-IX:19; NM-31 (1976); NM-15 (1992); 
NMH-239; Bruun-9172; Weight: 0.49 gms. A both handsome and extremely rare “White Rose” type named thus be -
cause of its relatively high silver content and the roses that adorn the quarters of the cross. The facing bust is clearly 
inspired by the contemporary English Sterlings.
€2,500-€3,500

========== PAGE 168 ==========

Page 150 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
12019
NORW AY . Penny, ND (after 1285). Eirik II Magnusson. NGC 
AU-50. Schive-IX:24; NM-29:1 (1976); NM-10 (1992); NMH-
234; Bruun-9176; Weight: 1.31 gms. Very slightly chipped but 
otherwise a most remarkable example with superior details.
With this issue Eirik (Eric) for a brief period managed to raise the 
standard of the Norwegian monetary issues to a reasonable level, 
and at the time this type was known as an “Eirik Gross”. Outright 
warfare with the Danes and constant feuding with the Catholic 
Church (earning him the nickname “Priesthater”) however soon 
debased the currency again. 
€2,000-€3,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
  
Photo Enlarged 2x
12020
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1290-95). Eirik II Magnusson. NGC 
EF-40. Schive-IX:41; NM-30/2 (1976); NM-14 (1992); NMH-
238; Bruun-9180; Weight: 1.22 gms. Rare in this superior condi-
tion.
This so-called “Svartkrone” carries the unusual Christian reverse 
legend: +:CRVX IhV: XPI (“The cross of Christ”). 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

Session II • Thursday , October 30, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Exquisite and Very Rare Ducal Crown Penny
12021
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1285-90). Oslo Mint. Håkon (V) Magnusson as Duke. NGC EF-40. Schive-X:5; NM-32:1 
(1976); NM-1 (1992); NMH-240; Bruun-9186; Weight: 1.37 gms. A very rare coin with an exquisite, dark patina and 
sharp details.
Håkon, king Eirik’s younger brother, ruled areas around Oslo and Stavanger as a duke from 1280 to 1299, with the right to 
issue coins of his own. After taking over the throne in 1299, Oslo gradually took over the functions of capital from Bergen. 
On this especially interesting variant, Haakon is wearing a Ducal crown in the shape of a wreath of roses.
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

(1976); NM-2 (1992); NMH-241; Bruun-9190; Weight: 0.70 gms. A most agreeable example of this scarce type with an 
early reference to Oslo as the Mint. (+MOnETA DE ASLOIE). 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Session II • Thursday , October 30, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Exceptionally Well-Struck and Rare
12025
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1299-1300). Oslo Mint (?). Håkon V Magnusson. NGC AU-55. Schive-X:23; NM-36 (1976); 
NM-1 (1992); NMH-246; Bruun-9196; Weight: 0.73 gms. Rare and exceptionally well-struck with unusually pleasing 
surfaces.
This type which was formerly believed to be a 1/2 Penny is known through the archives as a “ Albi Rosati” (White Roseate) 
that although of a lighter weight was struck with an increased fineness as a counterpart to the vehemently unpopular so-
called “Black Crown” Pennies. The design was copied from the Scottish Sterlings.
€4,000-€5,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

legible.

Lovely, Rare and Highly Interesting Bracteate Penny
12028
NORW AY . Penny, ND (1380-87). Oslo Mint (?). Olav IV Håkonsson. NGC AU-50. Schive-XII:51; NM-51 (1976); 
NM-1 (1992); NMH-277; Bruun-9217; Weight: 0.20 gms. Old numbering in ink at lower obverse. Rare and of excep-
tional condition for type.
This highly interesting bracteate type with a crowned O, was struck for the underaged Olav by his mother, Queen Regent 
Margrethe I. No coins are known to have been struck for Olav (Oluf II) in Denmark. Olav IV was the last Norwegian king 
to be born on Norwegian soil until Harald V who took over the throne in 1991.
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

toned example of this very rare and interesting type with the reverse motif being a medieval A for Ánslo, Asloensis (Oslo).

and were also legal tender in Denmark.

========== PAGE 176 ==========

Page 158 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
A Rare Survivor of Unsurpassable Beauty
12036
NORW AY . Skilling, ND (1510-1522). Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint. Erik Valkendorf. NGC MS-63. Galster-183; Sieg-
6; NMD-1; Schou-11; Schive-XVIII:5; Bruun-9261. Weight: 2.32 gms. Mintmaster: Fully lustrous, brilliantly struck 
and highly detailed. A “must be seen to be believed” survivor of unsurpassable beauty. Of the utmost rarity with only 
6 examples recorded of which this is the best. The other five are all in public collections: two in Denmark (National 
Museum), 2 in Norway (Bergen and Oslo) and one in Sweden (Royal Coin Cabinet).
Remarkably, the 6 known examples have been struck from five different sets of dies, indicating that the original mintage, 
which is believed to have taken place at the Bishop’s house, can’t have been completely insignificant. The fascinating obverse 
shows the Norwegian patron saint, Olaf Haraldsson trampling on a dragon, and on the reverse is seen the arms of the Dan-
ish noble family of Walkendorf surrounded by a legend with the name of the Archbishop.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex. Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Ole Devegge Collection (Copenhagen - 1851) Lot # 1164.

Splendid and Otherwise Unobtainable Hvid
12037
NORW AY . Hvid, ND (1510-22). Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint. Erik Valkendorf. NGC AU-58. Galster-184; Sieg-5; 
NMD-6; Schive-XVIII:13; Schou-14; Bruun-9262. Weight: 0.64 gms. A highly desirable piece, astoundingly well-struck 
and with traces of original luster. Among the late medieval Norwegian types, the Hvid from Erik Valkendorf has always 
stood out as something completely unobtainable. With only two other examples recorded, both in public collections in 
Copenhagen and Bergen, it is almost a miracle that the present example is not just there but actually wonderfully well 
preserved.
When Crown Prince Christian was sent to Norway as governor, Valkendorf accompanied him as chancellor, and when 
Gaute died in 1510, Valkendorf took over the position as Archbishop in Nidaros. Eventually Valkendorf however had a fall 
out with the now king Christian II and was forced into exile.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

========== PAGE 178 ==========

Page 160 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Very Rare and Historically Important Double Hvid
12038
NORW AY . Double Hvid, ND (1523-24). Oslo Mint. Hans Mule. NGC VF Details - Environmental Damage. Gal-
ster-165; Sieg-3; NMD-1; Schive-XV:24-26; Schou-1; Bruun-9264. Weight: 1.24 gms. Very rare and historically interest-
ing.
This highly debased issue was minted during the Interregnum from 1523 to 1524 by Hans Mule, the headsman of the for-
tress Akershus. Although Mule also functioned as the bishop of Oslo, he was known as a lecher and a violent tyrant. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 130 in his collection.
    
12039
NORW AY . Skilling, 1526. Oslo Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU-53. Galster-167; Sieg-2; NMD-3; Schive-XVI:5; Jensen & 
Skjoldager-N-13; Schou-1; Bruun-9268. Weight: 2.10 gms. Two very light splits in the flan near the edge aside, this is a 
most presentable example of this scarce issue. 
€3,000-€4,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 192 in his collection.

Charming Example of the Only Dated Bergen Skilling
12040
NORW AY . Skilling, 1530. Bergen Mint. Frederik I. NGC AU-55. Galster-169; Sieg-3; NMD-8; Schive-XVI:8-10; Jen-
sen & Skjoldager-N-05; Schou-1; Bruun-9271. Weight: 1.84 gms. An inviting example with a wonderfully clear date. 
Excessively rare and the only dated Skilling from Bergen from Frederik I.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 180 ==========

Page 162 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
    
12041
NORW AY . Skilling, ND (1524-33). Bergen Mint. Frederik I. NGC EF Details—Cleaned. Galster-170; Sieg-2; NMD-7; 
Schou-22; Jensen & Skjoldager-N-07; Bruun-9275. Weight: 1.88 gms. Mintmaster: Hans Hansen. A lovely example with 
incredible details and very rare thus.
This interesting emission was overseen by Vincens Lunge (1486-1537) who early in the reign of Frederik I was sent to North-
ern Norway to enforce the reign of the new king. 
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
12042
NORW AY . Skilling, 1535. Oslo Mint. Christian III. NGC VF Details—Bent. Galster-175; Sieg-1; NMD-1; Schou-1; 
Bruun-9291. Weight: 1.50 gms. A very rare and historically interesting issue minted for Christian as pretender to the 
throne (HERES NORVEGIE) by the lord of Akershus Castle, Erik Gyldenstierne. 
€2,000-€2,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Bruun owned two examples of this coin and it is no longer possible to tell if the present example was formerly in the Joh. G. Guildal 
Collection (# 229) or in the Countship of Brahesminde Collection.
    
12043
NORW AY . Skilling, ND (1522-38). Nidaros (Trondheim) Mint. Olav Engelbrektsson. NGC EF Details—Bent. Gal-
ster-187; Sieg-1; NMD-2; Schou-2; Bruun-9293. Weight: 1.49 gms. Mintmaster: Jacob Schult. A nice example of this 
interesting issue struck for the last Norwegian Archbishop, Olav Engelbrektsson.
Following his defeat against the Danish protestant king, Christian III, Engelbrektsson fled to Belgium bringing with him a 
number of valuable treasures from the Nidaros Cathedral. 
€1,200-€1,600
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 181 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 163
Session II • Thursday , October 30, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
Highly Attractive and Very Rare Gimsøy Mark
12044
NORW AY . Mark, 1545. Gimsøy Mint. Christian III. NGC EF-40. Hede-2; Sieg-3; NMD-5; Schou-7; Bruun-9301. 
Weight: 9.40 gms. Mintmaster: Jørgen Kock, the younger. A very attractive example with indigo and abricot hues en-
hancing the lovely cabinet toning. Extremely rare, with only three believed to exist in private collections, including the 
example sold in first sale of the L. E. Bruun collection (lot # 1173, EF-Details), which is however struck from different 
dies (Schou 6). The one not in the Bruun collection is holed.
This important but highly elusive type was struck at Gimsøy in the former nunnery, near Skien in Telemark county, presum-
ably from local silver mined at Gullnes. The mint was only in use from 1544 to 1546 when the buildings burned down and 
the silver mine in Gullnes dried out.
€30,000-€40,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 316 in his collection.

Page 164 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Beautiful Example of the First Norwegian Half Specie
12045
NORW AY . 1/2 Speciedaler, 1628. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Cleaned. KM-7; Hede-2; Sieg-
12; NMD-50; Schou-27; Bruun-9313. Weight: 12.76 gms. Mintmaster: Anders Pedersen. A beautifully toned example 
in a condition way above what is otherwise encountered in this extremely rare type, which was the first Half Taler to be 
minted in Norway.
€15,000-€20,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

12058
NORW AY . 4 Skilling, 1643. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU Details—Bent. KM-21.1a; Hede-15B; Sieg-4.2; 
NMD-127; Schou-40; Bruun-9470. Weight: 1.13 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner the Elder. A most pleasing “must-have” 
for any advanced collector of Norwegian minors from the 17th century. Excessively rare one-year type minted by Peter 
Grüner shortly after he had taken over the position from Anders Pedersen. 
€2,500-€3,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
12059
NORW AY . 2 Mark, 1644. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC AU-55+. KM-28; Hede-19; Sieg-7.2; NMD-113; 
Aagaard-TC1; Schou-55; Bruun-9489. Weight: 10.85 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner the Elder. Exceptionally full of eye-
appeal for type.
In the middle of the Torstenson War and possibly to invoke the help of God against the Swedes, King Christian personally 
sketched the coin design using the Tetragrammaton (the Hebrew four-letter-name of God) as a key element on silver and gold 
coins struck in Copenhagen, Christiania, and Glückstadt 
€1,000-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Frederik Chr. Bech Collection (Copenhagen - 1906) Lot # 339.
    
12060
NORW AY . 8 Skilling, 1644. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC MS-63. KM-26; H-14B; Sieg-5.3; NMD-124B; 
Schou-88; Bruun-9496. Weight: 2.32 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner the Elder. A stunning Choice example with a very 
appealing toning. Rare one-year sub-type with the mintmark (a trefoil) on both sides. 
€1,200-€1,500
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Joh. G. Guildal Collection (Private Sale - 1918); later published by the Danish Numismatic Society as item 991 in his collection.

========== PAGE 188 ==========

Page 170 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Illustrated in Most Major Reference Works on Norwegian Coins
12061
NORW AY . 4 Skilling (Pattern), 1644. Christiania Mint. Christian IV . NGC VF Details—Holed. KM-cf. PnB19 (in-
correctly listed as copper); Hede-21 (plate coin); Sieg-21 (plate coin); NMD-142 (plate coin); Schou-110 (plate coin); 
Bruun-9500. Weight: 1.01 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner the Elder. The contemporary hole only adds to the mystery 
surrounding this UNIQUE and highly important pattern illustrated in most major reference works on Norwegian coins. 
Three very different 4 Skilling patterns were struck in 1644, but none of them were set into production. All three are only 
know in one example with this one being the only one in private hands (the other two are in the National Museum of Den-
mark). The present is the only one bearing a portrait, while the other two have the Norwegian lion on the obverse and the 
denomination, IIII or IV on the reverse.
€5,000-€7,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

========== PAGE 189 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 171
Session II • Thursday , October 30, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT

H-7B; Sieg-18.2; NMD-22; Schou-13; Bruun-9511. Weight: 57.89 gms. Mintmaster: Peter Grüner the Elder. In spite of 
at some point having been lightly cleaned, this is a most engaging piece with lots of detail and luster embellished by a 
pretty toning at the rim. Of the utmost rarity in private ownership. 
€22,000-€26,000

Page 176 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Commanding and Fascinating Unique Triple Taler
A Once in a Lifetime Opportunity
 
12070
NORW AY . 3 Speciedaler, 1652-FG. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-A3589; KM-39; 
Hede-10B; Sieg-23.2; NMD-19; Aagaard-36.1; Schou-2; Bruun-9644. Weight: 86.17 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. 
Engraver: Hans zum Busch. Even though the Bruun collection abound with rarities of the highest caliber, this majestic 
Triple Speciedaler is almost in a league of its own being not only unique in private ownership but actually the only ex-
ample in existence. As the Double Speciedaler of the same type (Aagaard-37.1) is only known from public collections 
(Copenhagen, St. Peterburg), it is safe to say that this is a once in a lifetime opportunity to acquire a genuinely unique 
piece of Norwegian numismatic history. The fact that the condition for such a hefty coin is way superior to what could 
be expected does only add to its desirability.
€120,000-€160,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

12074
NORW AY . 1/2 Speciedaler, 1653. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Corrosion. KM-36; Hede-11C; 
Sieg-42.1; NMD-120; Schou-26; Aagaard-42.1; Bruun-9665. Weight: 13.76 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner (FG). Al-
though clearly a “find coin” , the portrait is remarkably strong. An excessively rare one-year type which is otherwise only 
known from the State Art Collections in Dresden and The Royal Norwegian Society of Sciences and Letters, Trondheim. 
€8,000-€12,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
HERMAN HENRIK JULIUS LYNGE  
(1822-1897) 
Herman Henrik Julius Lynge (1822-1897) is widely consid-
ered to be the father of the professional antiquarian book trade 
in Denmark. He obtained both the Danish and the Swedish 
medal of honor, and when he turned 70, he was awarded the 
title of "Kancelliråd" (titulary chancellor). His estate auction 
took place in 1899 and was divided into 5 small catalogs com-
prising a total of several thousand coins, medals, tokens and 
paper money.


========== PAGE 198 ==========

Page 180 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Of the Utmost Rarity and Importance
12075
NORW AY . 1/8 Speciedaler, 1654. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC EF-45. KM-49; Hede-13B; Sieg-9.2; NMD-143; 
Schou-11; Aagaard-35.1; Bruun-9686. Weight: 3.41 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. Engraver: Hans zum Busch. An 
immensely inviting piece, sharply struck on an even flan and exhibiting a delightful cabinet toning. A one year type of 
the utmost rarity and importance with the only other recorded example being the one at the Danish National Museum.
At a brief glance this type look similar to the equally rare dates of 1649 and 1650, but between the rear legs of the Norwegian 
lion can be seen Frederik Grüner’s initials. Following the death of his father, Peter Grüner, Frederik took over the position 
as a Mintmaster in January 1651.
€10,000-€15,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

12076
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1655. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58. Dav-3595; cf. KM-48; Hede-17; Sieg-26; 
NMD-77; Aagaard-45.8 (54-5/55-7); Schou-11; Bruun-9697. Weight: 28.63 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. Engraver: 
Jeremias Hercules (obverse). An intense luster surrounds the commanding portrait of the king making this a very desir-
able piece. 
€6,000-€8,000

Double Taler Worthy of the Most Discerning Collections
12078
NORW AY . 2 Speciedaler, 1657-FG. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-53. Dav-3596; KM-41; Hede-22; Sieg-
30; NMD-44; Aagaard-49.4; Schou-13; Bruun-9734. Weight: 57.59 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. With less than 
a handful examples known, a sharp strike and lustrous fields showing only light surface marks, this is certainly a piece 
worthy of the most discerning collections. 
An amusing detail can be seen on the portrait where the engraver’s centering point looks like a wart on the king’s chin.
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: C. F. Schubart (Copenhagen - 1831) Lot # 1105.

Gorgeous Double Taler of the Utmost Rarity
12079
NORW AY . 2 Speciedaler, 1658-FG. Christiania mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Reverse Graffiti. Dav-3600; 
KM-42; Hede-27; Sieg-34; NMD-46; Aagaard-54.7; Schou-10; Bruun-9764. Weight: 57.41 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik 
Grüner. Engraver: Matthias Först (?). A simple cross shaped graffito in the upper reverse field hardly deter from the 
desirability of this monumental piece that boasts a strong portrait and a high degree of details. Very few if indeed any 
other examples in private ownership.

From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
    
12080
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1658. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58. Dav-3603; KM-A43; Hede-30A; Sieg-37.1; 
NMD-84; Aagaard-57.2; Schou-18; Bruun-9769. Weight: 28.66 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. Engraver: Matthias 
Först (?) (obverse); Johan Blum (reverse). A very rare one-year type in a delightful condition that really shows of the 
many elaborate details, for instance in the frame surrounding the rampant lion.

Page 184 Stack’s Bowers Galleries
The L.  E. Bruun Col lection • A Corpus of Scandinavian Monetary History Part III
Amazing Double Taler of Paramount Rarity
12081
NORW AY . 2 Speciedaler, 1659. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Cleaned. Dav-3606; KM-A52; 
Hede-34; Sieg-41; NMD-49; Aagaard-69.2; Schou-16; Bruun-9796. Weight: 57.38 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. 
Engraver: Johan Blum (obverse). Beautifully struck with excellent details including the lion head on the king’s shoulder. 
The only other example known in private collections which also belonged to Bruun (Sale II, lot 14054) was struck from 
a different obverse die with NOR in the legend instead of NORVE.
Two small scratches in the obverse right field are presumably contemporary and should be read as the Roman numeral II 
signifying a Double Taler. 
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
Ex: Georg F. Timm Collection (Copenhagen - 1831) Lot # 906.
GEORG FRIEDERICH TIMM  
(1746-1829)  
Georg Friederich Timm (1746-1829) worked as a metalworker at the 
royal court and had a highly interesting collection of coins and med-
als, including many rarities. The catalog from his estate auction in 
1831 was written by two of the most prominent numismatists of the 
time, Ole Devegge and Christian Jürgensen Thomsen, and is still of 
considerable scientific value.


========== PAGE 203 ==========

 Bid Online at www.StacksBowers.com Pa ge 185
Session II • Thursday , October 30, 2025 • 2:00  PM CET / 6:00  AM PT
    
12082
NORW AY . Speciedaler, 1659. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU-58. Dav-3607; KM-54; Hede-33B; Sieg-40.2; 
NMD-89B; Aagaard-70.1; Schou-20; Bruun-9799. Weight: 28.72 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. Engraver: Johan 
Blum. An amazing example with especially the reverse showing full Mint State luster. 
€4,000-€6,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: H. H. J. Lynge Collection (Copenhagen - 1899) Lot # 1209.
Highly Detailed and Extremely Rare Half Taler
12083
NORW AY . 1/2 Speciedaler, 1659. Christiania Mint. Frederik III. NGC AU Details—Reverse Cleaned. KM-53; 
Hede-37; Sieg-18; NMD-127; Schou-29; Aagaard-71.1; Bruun-9803. Weight: 14.39 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner 
(FG). Engraver: Johan Blum (obverse). A hairline scratch in obverse field aside, this is a lovely piece with a very clear 
strike. Extremely rare.
The Norwegian Half Talers struck during the reign of Frederik III are each and every one very rare. It is therefore no wonder 
that the standard question the famous Danish collector, Axel Ernst (1891 - 1964) put forward to collectors annoyingly brag-
ging about their Norwegian collections was: "How many 1/2 Speciedalers from Frederik III do you own?".
€25,000-€35,000
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).

NMD-93; Aagaard-79.1 (61-2/62-9); Schou-24; Bruun-9859. Weight: 28.67 gms. Mintmaster: Frederik Grüner. Engrav-
er: Johan Blum (obverse). A splendid example with an excessively rare portrait (Type XI) only known from one die, 
which was re-used in 1662. 
€8,000-€10,000
